message.content:
You are in the beta clone, checked out on branch bv-conntrack-20260604-BVAAF — the ₣BV conntrack-adjudication debug branch, based on the
  requal wrap 80b681e62. Your only task right now is to land the refined docket for pace ₢BVAAF onto this branch via a JJK reslate, then push.
  Do not mount, build, kludge, or run anything else.

  Steps:
  1. jjx_open (alone, with your model id e.g. claude-opus-4-8). Use the returned ☉-id on all later jjx calls.
  2. Optional sanity: jjx_orient with {"firemark":"BV"} to confirm ₢BVAAF exists on this branch with its current (pre-refinement) docket.
  3. Write the officium's gazette_in.md (path .claude/jjm/officia/<id-without-☉>/gazette_in.md) with exactly the content between the markers
  below, then call jjx_redocket with {}. The # jjezs_reslate BVAAF line is the wire-format lede — one bare token, no ₢ glyph — followed by the
  docket body.
  4. After it reports Revised 1 pace(s), push: git push origin bv-conntrack-20260604-BVAAF.
  5. Report the new branch tip and stop. (This is a single-pace gallops change — it must be the only modification; don't touch code.)

  === BEGIN gazette_in.md (verbatim) ===
  # jjezs_reslate BVAAF

  ## Character
  Investigation → conditional fix; security-relevant. Reconfirm the platform split first, then adjudicate.

  Substrate: all execution rides branch bv-conntrack-20260604-BVAAF (off the requal wrap 80b681e62); git is the only transport to the rocket
  funduses (wsl@rocket, native docker; cygwin@rocket, Docker Desktop). Before each run, force the fundus to the branch's pushed tip via the
  paddock's sanctioned hard-sync to origin/bv-conntrack-20260604-BVAAF.

  Integrity under kludge: siege kludges host-local on the fundus and self-notches a stamp commit there, so every run mutates the branch. Keep
  it single-lined — pull --rebase the fundus commit into the curia and push before the next fundus force-syncs, so WSL and Cygwin always land
  on the same integral tip. (Fundus needs git user.name/email or the kludge notch dies exit 128 — paddock.)

  Reconfirm: run siege (kludge-tadmor + tadmor crucible) on WSL first, then Cygwin DD, and compare the conntrack_spoofed_ack verdict. A clean
  split (WSL green; Cygwin 45/46, the recorded baseline) is platform divergence; a breach on both is a latent egress gap.

  Mechanism for the adjudication — sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack (rbida_sorties.rs) + Memos/memo-20260603-cygwin-ifrit-first-run-conntrack.md:
  the "response" is a RST from the allowed host returning through a conntrack entry the lone ACK created (nf_conntrack_tcp_loose=1 on the
  shared WSL2 kernel, host value confirmed on wsl@rocket), past a dest-based (not state-gated) allowlist in the sentry FORWARD chain
  (common-sentry-context/rbjs_sentry.sh).

  ## Done
  Verdict explained (divergence vs real gap), grounded in the cross-platform verdicts. If a real gap: sentry egress hardened (e.g. tcp_loose=0,
  drop INVALID, or state-gate the allowlist) and re-tested green on both platforms. If divergence: documented with rationale and the suite
  verdict reconciled across backends.
  === END gazette_in.md ===

addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

# jjezs_reslate BVAAF

## Character
Investigation → conditional fix; security-relevant. Reconfirm the platform split first, then adjudicate.

Substrate: all execution rides branch bv-conntrack-20260604-BVAAF (off the requal wrap 80b681e62); git is the only transport to the rocket funduses (wsl@rocket, native docker; cygwin@rocket, Docker Desktop). Before each run, force the fundus to the branch's pushed tip via the paddock's sanctioned hard-sync to origin/bv-conntrack-20260604-BVAAF.

Integrity under kludge: siege kludges host-local on the fundus and self-notches a stamp commit there, so every run mutates the branch. Keep it single-lined — pull --rebase the fundus commit into the curia and push before the next fundus force-syncs, so WSL and Cygwin always land on the same integral tip. (Fundus needs git user.name/email or the kludge notch dies exit 128 — paddock.)

Reconfirm: run siege (kludge-tadmor + tadmor crucible) on WSL first, then Cygwin DD, and compare the conntrack_spoofed_ack verdict. A clean split (WSL green; Cygwin 45/46, the recorded baseline) is platform divergence; a breach on both is a latent egress gap.

Mechanism for the adjudication — sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack (rbida_sorties.rs) + Memos/memo-20260603-cygwin-ifrit-first-run-conntrack.md: the "response" is a RST from the allowed host returning through a conntrack entry the lone ACK created (nf_conntrack_tcp_loose=1 on the shared WSL2 kernel, host value confirmed on wsl@rocket), past a dest-based (not state-gated) allowlist in the sentry FORWARD chain (common-sentry-context/rbjs_sentry.sh).

## Done
Verdict explained (divergence vs real gap), grounded in the cross-platform verdicts. If a real gap: sentry egress hardened (e.g. tcp_loose=0, drop INVALID, or state-gate the allowlist) and re-tested green on both platforms. If divergence: documented with rationale and the suite verdict reconciled across backends.

+

content.text: Now let me read the paddock from the gazette and the referenced source files to ground the work.

content.content:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BV
2	
3	## Shape
4	
5	Prove that Recipe Bottle's test harness (theurge) can run on an **uncontrolled**
6	Cygwin — a stock Windows+Cygwin box we do not provision. This is a consumer-
7	credibility claim: a consumer can qualify their own install on their own machine
8	and trust the verdict. It is deliberately distinct from the `rbk-NN-win-*` heats,
9	which are all controlled-infrastructure work (formal BURN garrison, remote
10	control, depot regime) on machines we own.
11	
12	## Progress & scope (updated 2026-06-03)
13	
14	The heat outgrew its original fast/validation-tier scope: the **crucible tier now
15	runs on uncontrolled Cygwin Docker Desktop**. siege (the renamed tadmor suite)
16	charges and runs the adversarial sortie set 45/46; blockade (moriah, airgap)
17	follows. The two membranes that made it possible — the compose-CLI path
18	normalizer and the nested bind-mountpoint pre-creation — are documented in
19	`Memos/memo-20260603-windows-docker-desktop-bind-mount.md`. This supersedes the
20	"container tiers run on WSL only / fast-tier only" locked decision below. One
21	open finding: the `conntrack_spoofed_ack` sortie BREACHED on siege; adjudication
22	pending (`Memos/memo-20260603-cygwin-ifrit-first-run-conntrack.md`).
23	
24	## Remote build discipline (git is the filesystem)
25	
26	All work runs on a remote fundus (`cygwin@rocket`); code reaches it through git,
27	never scp. The cycle, learned the hard way this heat:
28	
29	1. Edit on the curia (this station).
30	2. Notch (`jjx_record`) — a real commit; the affiliation is the transport label.
31	3. `git pull --rebase` then `git push` on the curia.
32	4. On the fundus: `git pull` to fast-forward — or, when the fundus tree is dirty
33	   or detached (a prior `jjx_plant` or scratch state), `git fetch && git checkout
34	   -f main && git reset --hard origin/main`. Destructive git is sanctioned here
35	   only because it replaces with byte-identical committed content.
36	5. Run the tabtarget on the fundus over ssh. No trailing `echo` — it masks the
37	   exit code; let ssh propagate the tabtarget's status.
38	
39	Two traps that cost time this heat:
40	
41	- **scp is wrong.** It dirties the fundus tree and diverges from git. Git is the
42	  only sanctioned transport for code.
43	- **The fundus needs a git identity.** Without `git config user.name/email` on
44	  the fundus, any fixture that owns its own notch (kludge-tadmor and friends)
45	  dies with `Author identity unknown` (exit 128). Set it once per host.
46	
47	Exceptions and adjuncts:
48	
49	- **Kludge-mode crucibles cannot ride git-as-filesystem.** Kludge images are
50	  host-local — the image must be built on the fundus. So kludge on the fundus and
51	  let the fixture commit the stamp there; accept the temporary cygwin-resident
52	  stamp pollution. (Conjure-mode / blockade summons from GAR, so it rides git
53	  normally.)
54	- **Shared-depot credentials go stale.** Another host's governor mantle obsoletes
55	  this fundus's Director/Retriever SAs — they fail with `Invalid JWT Signature`
56	  (persisting past the retry budget, not a real propagation race). Re-establish
57	  before any GAR-touching run: reinstall the Payor OAuth, then run `dogfight`
58	  (its `canonical-invest` re-mantles the Governor and re-invests
59	  Retriever+Director).
60	
61	## Central thesis (the real problem)
62	
63	The build system is not the hard part — **the bash-launch boundary is.** RBTDRX
64	(`rbtdrx_platform.rs`) already solves the *path-string* boundary at theurge's
65	Rust↔bash crossing, and it is sound: it keys on `OSTYPE=cygwin`, which Cygwin
66	bash exports to spawned children (verified on `cygwin@rocket`). But RBTDRX does
67	**not** solve the *process-launch* boundary, and that is where the wall is:
68	
69	- A Windows-native binary (which `x86_64-pc-windows-gnu` produces) **cannot**
70	  `CreateProcess` a `.sh` directly — Rust's `std::process::Command` on Windows
71	  supports only `.exe` (undocumented `.bat`/`.cmd`); a `.sh` yields "not a valid
72	  Win32 application." This is a documented Rust limitation, not a bug to wait out.
73	- theurge's invocation layer is inconsistent: some paths route through
74	  `bash -c` (correct), but the tabtarget-invocation primitives
75	  (`rbtdri_invoke_impl`, `rbtdrf_run_tt`) exec the `.sh` directly via
76	  `Command::new(tabtarget)`. Those paths cannot have run green on Cygwin —
77	  RBTDRX is forward-looking scaffolding and the Cygwin bring-up is unfinished.
78	
79	## Locked decisions
80	
81	- **Commit to Route A first — not route-neutral.** Build with the
82	  already-installed `stable-x86_64-pc-windows-gnu` toolchain (rustc 1.95.0 at
83	  `/cygdrive/c/Users/cygwin/.cargo/bin`; theurge's deps are pure-Rust, so no C
84	  toolchain / no system libs needed), and **finish the bring-up by routing every
85	  tabtarget invocation through `bash` instead of exec'ing the `.sh` directly.**
86	  RBTDRX stays and handles the path strings.
87	- **Route C is the documented fallback, not the opening move.** A first-class
88	  `x86_64-pc-cygwin` target exists (Rust 1.86+, Tier 3) and is semantically the
89	  "correct" answer — but it ships no precompiled std (needs nightly `-Z
90	  build-std`), and requires Cygwin `gcc` + LLVM ≥20.1. Fall back to it only if
91	  the native-binary path translation proves too leaky to finish Route A.
92	- **Toolchain installs are deferred to the experiment, not the dep pace.** The
93	  dep-install pace carries only route-independent knowns; `gcc`/`rust`/LLVM are
94	  Route-C-specific and must not be installed until/unless Route A is abandoned.
95	- **Scope is the runtime-free tier.** Container tiers (service/crucible) need a
96	  real container runtime; on Cygwin the only `docker` is Windows Docker Desktop,
97	  with a separate bind-mount path-translation wall RBTDRX does not cover.
98	  Per existing discipline, container tests run on the WSL side. This heat
99	  targets the **fast/validation** tier only.
100	  *(Superseded 2026-06-03 — see "Progress & scope" above: the crucible tier now
101	  runs on Cygwin Docker Desktop via the two Windows-docker membranes.)*
102	
103	## What done looks like
104	
105	`tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.fast.sh` runs green over `cygwin@rocket`. A legitimate
106	alternative outcome is honest: the launch-boundary fix is proven and the
107	remaining work scoped, with heavier surgery deferred — the experiment is
108	exploratory and may reveal more than the release timeline wants to absorb.
109	
110	## Known gaps to close
111	
112	- **PATH visibility.** `cargo` exists on the host but is not on the
113	  non-interactive ssh command channel's PATH (rustup wired the Windows user
114	  PATH; the sshd-spawned shell that runs tabtargets does not see it). Make
115	  `.cargo/bin` resolvable to that shell — most robustly via the Windows *system*
116	  PATH or `~/.ssh/environment`.
117	- **The launch-boundary code change** in `rbtdri`/`rbtdrf` (above).
118	- **shellcheck path-argument boundary (deferred — off the fast-tier critical
119	  path).** `shellcheck` has no native Cygwin package (Haskell/GHC is
120	  incompatible with Cygwin's path layout); the install is the official Windows
121	  `shellcheck.exe` in `/usr/local/bin` (admin-placed — the unprivileged account
122	  cannot write there). It resolves and runs, but as a Windows-native tool it
123	  cannot open Cygwin `/`-style path arguments (`openBinaryFile: does not
124	  exist`) — it needs Windows-form paths (`cygpath -w`) or stdin. This is
125	  RBTDRX-family path translation but a **distinct site** from the `.sh`-launch
126	  boundary above: a Windows *tool* receiving Cygwin path *arguments*, not a
127	  `.sh` being `CreateProcess`'d. It surfaces only when the harness drives
128	  shellcheck in `rbw-tr` QualifyRelease — never in the fast/validation tier this
129	  heat targets. Close it if/when QualifyRelease is brought to Cygwin.
130	
131	# jjezs_pace ₢BVAAF
132	
133	## Character
134	Investigation → conditional fix; security-relevant. Reconfirm the platform split first, then adjudicate.
135	
136	Substrate: all execution rides branch bv-conntrack-20260604-BVAAF (off the requal wrap 80b681e62); git is the only transport to the rocket funduses (wsl@rocket, native docker; cygwin@rocket, Docker Desktop). Before each run, force the fundus to the branch's pushed tip via the paddock's sanctioned hard-sync to origin/bv-conntrack-20260604-BVAAF.
137	
138	Integrity under kludge: siege kludges host-local on the fundus and self-notches a stamp commit there, so every run mutates the branch. Keep it single-lined — pull --rebase the fundus commit into the curia and push before the next fundus force-syncs, so WSL and Cygwin always land on the same integral tip. (Fundus needs git user.name/email or the kludge notch dies exit 128 — paddock.)
139	
140	Reconfirm: run siege (kludge-tadmor + tadmor crucible) on WSL first, then Cygwin DD, and compare the conntrack_spoofed_ack verdict. A clean split (WSL green; Cygwin 45/46, the recorded baseline) is platform divergence; a breach on both is a latent egress gap.
141	
142	Mechanism for the adjudication — sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack (rbida_sorties.rs) + Memos/memo-20260603-cygwin-ifrit-first-run-conntrack.md: the "response" is a RST from the allowed host returning through a conntrack entry the lone ACK created (nf_conntrack_tcp_loose=1 on the shared WSL2 kernel, host value confirmed on wsl@rocket), past a dest-based (not state-gated) allowlist in the sentry FORWARD chain (common-sentry-context/rbjs_sentry.sh).
143	
144	## Done
145	Verdict explained (divergence vs real gap), grounded in the cross-platform verdicts. If a real gap: sentry egress hardened (e.g. tcp_loose=0, drop INVALID, or state-gate the allowlist) and re-tested green on both platforms. If divergence: documented with rationale and the suite verdict reconciled across backends.
146

follows. The two membranes that made it possible — the compose-CLI path
normalizer and the nested bind-mountpoint pre-creation — are documented in
`Memos/memo-20260603-windows-docker-desktop-bind-mount.md`. This supersedes the
"container tiers run on WSL only / fast-tier only" locked decision below. One
open finding: the `conntrack_spoofed_ack` sortie BREACHED on siege; adjudication
pending (`Memos/memo-20260603-cygwin-ifrit-first-run-conntrack.md`).

## Remote build discipline (git is the filesystem)

All work runs on a remote fundus (`cygwin@rocket`); code reaches it through git,
never scp. The cycle, learned the hard way this heat:

1. Edit on the curia (this station).
2. Notch (`jjx_record`) — a real commit; the affiliation is the transport label.
3. `git pull --rebase` then `git push` on the curia.
4. On the fundus: `git pull` to fast-forward — or, when the fundus tree is dirty
   or detached (a prior `jjx_plant` or scratch state), `git fetch && git checkout
   -f main && git reset --hard origin/main`. Destructive git is sanctioned here
   only because it replaces with byte-identical committed content.
5. Run the tabtarget on the fundus over ssh. No trailing `echo` — it masks the
   exit code; let ssh propagate the tabtarget's status.

Two traps that cost time this heat:

- **scp is wrong.** It dirties the fundus tree and diverges from git. Git is the
  only sanctioned transport for code.
- **The fundus needs a git identity.** Without `git config user.name/email` on
  the fundus, any fixture that owns its own notch (kludge-tadmor and friends)
  dies with `Author identity unknown` (exit 128). Set it once per host.

Exceptions and adjuncts:

- **Kludge-mode crucibles cannot ride git-as-filesystem.** Kludge images are
  host-local — the image must be built on the fundus. So kludge on the fundus and
  let the fixture commit the stamp there; accept the temporary cygwin-resident
  stamp pollution. (Conjure-mode / blockade summons from GAR, so it rides git
  normally.)
- **Shared-depot credentials go stale.** Another host's governor mantle obsoletes
  this fundus's Director/Retriever SAs — they fail with `Invalid JWT Signature`
  (persisting past the retry budget, not a real propagation race). Re-establish
  before any GAR-touching run: reinstall the Payor OAuth, then run `dogfight`
  (its `canonical-invest` re-mantles the Governor and re-invests
  Retriever+Director).

## Central thesis (the real problem)

The build system is not the hard part — **the bash-launch boundary is.** RBTDRX
(`rbtdrx_platform.rs`) already solves the *path-string* boundary at theurge's
Rust↔bash crossing, and it is sound: it keys on `OSTYPE=cygwin`, which Cygwin
bash exports to spawned children (verified on `cygwin@rocket`). But RBTDRX does
**not** solve the *process-launch* boundary, and that is where the wall is:

- A Windows-native binary (which `x86_64-pc-windows-gnu` produces) **cannot**
  `CreateProcess` a `.sh` directly — Rust's `std::process::Command` on Windows
  supports only `.exe` (undocumented `.bat`/`.cmd`); a `.sh` yields "not a valid
  Win32 application." This is a documented Rust limitation, not a bug to wait out.
- theurge's invocation layer is inconsistent: some paths route through
  `bash -c` (correct), but the tabtarget-invocation primitives
  (`rbtdri_invoke_impl`, `rbtdrf_run_tt`) exec the `.sh` directly via
  `Command::new(tabtarget)`. Those paths cannot have run green on Cygwin —
  RBTDRX is forward-looking scaffolding and the Cygwin bring-up is unfinished.

## Locked decisions

- **Commit to Route A first — not route-neutral.** Build with the
  already-installed `stable-x86_64-pc-windows-gnu` toolchain (rustc 1.95.0 at
  `/cygdrive/c/Users/cygwin/.cargo/bin`; theurge's deps are pure-Rust, so no C
  toolchain / no system libs needed), and **finish the bring-up by routing every
  tabtarget invocation through `bash` instead of exec'ing the `.sh` directly.**
  RBTDRX stays and handles the path strings.
- **Route C is the documented fallback, not the opening move.** A first-class
  `x86_64-pc-cygwin` target exists (Rust 1.86+, Tier 3) and is semantically the
  "correct" answer — but it ships no precompiled std (needs nightly `-Z
  build-std`), and requires Cygwin `gcc` + LLVM ≥20.1. Fall back to it only if
  the native-binary path translation proves too leaky to finish Route A.
- **Toolchain installs are deferred to the experiment, not the dep pace.** The
  dep-install pace carries only route-independent knowns; `gcc`/`rust`/LLVM are
  Route-C-specific and must not be installed until/unless Route A is abandoned.
- **Scope is the runtime-free tier.** Container tiers (service/crucible) need a
  real container runtime; on Cygwin the only `docker` is Windows Docker Desktop,
  with a separate bind-mount path-translation wall RBTDRX does not cover.
  Per existing discipline, container tests run on the WSL side. This heat
  targets the **fast/validation** tier only.
  *(Superseded 2026-06-03 — see "Progress & scope" above: the crucible tier now
  runs on Cygwin Docker Desktop via the two Windows-docker membranes.)*

## What done looks like

`tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.fast.sh` runs green over `cygwin@rocket`. A legitimate
alternative outcome is honest: the launch-boundary fix is proven and the
remaining work scoped, with heavier surgery deferred — the experiment is
exploratory and may reveal more than the release timeline wants to absorb.

## Known gaps to close

- **PATH visibility.** `cargo` exists on the host but is not on the
  non-interactive ssh command channel's PATH (rustup wired the Windows user
  PATH; the sshd-spawned shell that runs tabtargets does not see it). Make
  `.cargo/bin` resolvable to that shell — most robustly via the Windows *system*
  PATH or `~/.ssh/environment`.
- **The launch-boundary code change** in `rbtdri`/`rbtdrf` (above).
- **shellcheck path-argument boundary (deferred — off the fast-tier critical
  path).** `shellcheck` has no native Cygwin package (Haskell/GHC is
  incompatible with Cygwin's path layout); the install is the official Windows
  `shellcheck.exe` in `/usr/local/bin` (admin-placed — the unprivileged account
  cannot write there). It resolves and runs, but as a Windows-native tool it
  cannot open Cygwin `/`-style path arguments (`openBinaryFile: does not
  exist`) — it needs Windows-form paths (`cygpath -w`) or stdin. This is
  RBTDRX-family path translation but a **distinct site** from the `.sh`-launch
  boundary above: a Windows *tool* receiving Cygwin path *arguments*, not a
  `.sh` being `CreateProcess`'d. It surfaces only when the harness drives
  shellcheck in `rbw-tr` QualifyRelease — never in the fast/validation tier this
  heat targets. Close it if/when QualifyRelease is brought to Cygwin.

# jjezs_pace ₢BVAAF

## Character
Investigation → conditional fix; security-relevant. Reconfirm the platform split first, then adjudicate.

Substrate: all execution rides branch bv-conntrack-20260604-BVAAF (off the requal wrap 80b681e62); git is the only transport to the rocket funduses (wsl@rocket, native docker; cygwin@rocket, Docker Desktop). Before each run, force the fundus to the branch's pushed tip via the paddock's sanctioned hard-sync to origin/bv-conntrack-20260604-BVAAF.

Integrity under kludge: siege kludges host-local on the fundus and self-notches a stamp commit there, so every run mutates the branch. Keep it single-lined — pull --rebase the fundus commit into the curia and push before the next fundus force-syncs, so WSL and Cygwin always land on the same integral tip. (Fundus needs git user.name/email or the kludge notch dies exit 128 — paddock.)

Reconfirm: run siege (kludge-tadmor + tadmor crucible) on WSL first, then Cygwin DD, and compare the conntrack_spoofed_ack verdict. A clean split (WSL green; Cygwin 45/46, the recorded baseline) is platform divergence; a breach on both is a latent egress gap.

Mechanism for the adjudication — sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack (rbida_sorties.rs) + Memos/memo-20260603-cygwin-ifrit-first-run-conntrack.md: the "response" is a RST from the allowed host returning through a conntrack entry the lone ACK created (nf_conntrack_tcp_loose=1 on the shared WSL2 kernel, host value confirmed on wsl@rocket), past a dest-based (not state-gated) allowlist in the sentry FORWARD chain (common-sentry-context/rbjs_sentry.sh).

## Done
Verdict explained (divergence vs real gap), grounded in the cross-platform verdicts. If a real gap: sentry egress hardened (e.g. tcp_loose=0, drop INVALID, or state-gate the allowlist) and re-tested green on both platforms. If divergence: documented with rationale and the suite verdict reconciled across backends.

lastPrompt: mount and proceed.

content.content:
1	# First ifrit run on uncontrolled Cygwin — siege 45/46 and the conntrack_spoofed_ack breach
2	
3	*2026-06-03. Heat ₣BV (cygwin-spike). This was the **first time the ifrit
4	adversarial security suite ran on an uncontrolled Cygwin + Docker Desktop
5	host** (`cygwin@rocket`).*
6	
7	## Headline
8	
9	The `siege` suite (renamed this session from `tadmor` = `kludge-tadmor` +
10	`tadmor` crucible fixture) charged on Cygwin Docker Desktop and ran the full
11	adversarial sortie set: **45 passed, 1 failed (46 total)**, clean quench.
12	That is the heat's headline essentially achieved — theurge's security suite
13	runs, and 45 escape vectors are correctly contained, on a box we do not
14	provision.
15	
16	The single failure is recorded below. It does not diminish the result, but it
17	is a genuine finding that needs adjudication, **not** dismissal.
18	
19	## The one breach
20	
21	```
22	FAILED: rbtdrc_sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack
23	BREACH: spoofed ACK to 192.0.46.9:80 got response —
24	        conntrack RELATED,ESTABLISHED not filtering stateless ACKs
25	IFRIT_VERDICT: FAIL BREACH ...
26	```
27	
28	Sortie: `rbtdrc_sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack` →
29	`rbtdrc_invoke_ifrit(ctx, "conntrack-spoofed-ack", ...)` — an ifrit sortie
30	driven by the `rbtid` adjutant. (Full packet/source construction lives in the
31	sortie script; not yet read — see Next Steps.)
32	
33	Sentry FORWARD chain at the time (from the captured `iptables-rules.txt`):
34	
35	```
36	-P FORWARD DROP
37	-A FORWARD -m state --state RELATED,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT
38	-A FORWARD -j RBM-FORWARD
39	...
40	-A RBM-FORWARD -d 192.0.32.0/20 -i eth0 -j ACCEPT      # allowlisted CIDR
41	```
42	
43	## The crucial nuance — do not over-read this as a clean conntrack failure
44	
45	The target **`192.0.46.9` is inside `192.0.32.0/20`**, which is an
46	**allowlisted** destination in the FORWARD chain. So a packet to it is permitted
47	**by the dest-based allowlist rule**, independent of conntrack state. The
48	sortie's verdict attributes the breach purely to "conntrack RELATED,ESTABLISHED
49	not filtering stateless ACKs," but that attribution is **incomplete**: to an
50	allowed dest, the allowlist alone would let the packet through.
51	
52	Two non-exclusive readings, both worth testing:
53	
54	1. **Defense-in-depth expectation** — the sortie asserts that a *stateless*
55	   forged ACK should not elicit a response *even to an allowed host* (only a
56	   properly established connection should). The current rules are dest-based,
57	   not state-gated, for allowed CIDRs.
58	2. **`nf_conntrack_tcp_loose=1`** — the kernel default makes conntrack adopt a
59	   lone mid-stream ACK as ESTABLISHED, so the top `RELATED,ESTABLISHED` rule
60	   accepts it before the allowlist is even consulted.
61	
62	## The open question (platform divergence vs latent gap)
63	
64	Does this breach reproduce on **native Linux / WSL** (e.g. `cerebro`)?
65	
66	- **If it PASSES on native Linux but FAILS on Cygwin DD-WSL2** → a **platform
67	  verdict-divergence**: the Docker Desktop WSL2 / LinuxKit network stack handles
68	  conntrack (or `nf_conntrack_tcp_loose`) differently than native netfilter.
69	  This is a direct hit on the consumer-credibility claim — "a consumer can
70	  qualify their own install on their own machine and **trust the verdict**" —
71	  because the same suite would render a different security verdict depending on
72	  the host's docker backend.
73	- **If it FAILS everywhere** → a **latent property of the current sentry rules**
74	  that the cygwin run merely surfaced first (this suite had never run on cygwin
75	  before). Then it is a real egress-hardening item independent of platform.
76	
77	Either outcome matters; we cannot tell which from this single run.
78	
79	## Next steps to adjudicate
80	
81	1. Read the `conntrack-spoofed-ack` sortie script in `rbtid` — exact packet
82	   construction (is the source spoofed? is the response in-band or via a
83	   conntrack-created return path?) and the precise verdict logic.
84	2. Run `siege` (and/or the `tadmor` crucible fixture) on **`cerebro`** (native
85	   Linux) and on the **WSL** side of `rocket` — compare the
86	   `conntrack_spoofed_ack` verdict across backends.
87	3. Compare `nf_conntrack_tcp_loose` (and related conntrack sysctls) between the
88	   DD-WSL2 kernel and native Linux.
89	4. If a real gap: harden the sentry egress — e.g. set `nf_conntrack_tcp_loose=0`,
90	   add `-m conntrack --ctstate INVALID -j DROP`, and/or state-gate the allowlist
91	   so allowed CIDRs still require an established connection.
92	
93	## Provenance
94	
95	- Run trace (cygwin): `temp-buk/temp-20260603-050730-21760-358/rbtd/`
96	  (`rbtdrc_sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack/{trace.txt,bark-stdout.txt}`,
97	  `rbtdrc_sentry_iptables_loaded/iptables-rules.txt`). Temp dirs are
98	  operator-mutable and may be reaped; the salient lines are quoted above.
99	- The bottle/crucible only charged on cygwin at all because of the two
100	  Windows-Docker-Desktop membranes — see
101	  `memo-20260603-windows-docker-desktop-bind-mount.md`.
102	
103	## Addendum (2026-06-03, after blockade)
104	
105	blockade (moriah — conjure-mode, GAR-summoned, airgap-built) ran on Cygwin and
106	produced the **identical 45/46** — the same single `conntrack_spoofed_ack`
107	BREACH. moriah carries a runtime egress allowlist too (`tcp443_allow_example`
108	and `cidr_all_ports_allowed` both passed), so the breach reproduces
109	**cross-nameplate** on Cygwin DD — it is *not* asymmetric between the tether and
110	airgap nameplates. That narrows the cause toward the shared sentry-rule /
111	conntrack property and makes the **native-Linux/WSL reproduction the decisive
112	next test**: does it breach there too?

# First ifrit run on uncontrolled Cygwin — siege 45/46 and the conntrack_spoofed_ack breach

*2026-06-03. Heat ₣BV (cygwin-spike). This was the **first time the ifrit
adversarial security suite ran on an uncontrolled Cygwin + Docker Desktop
host** (`cygwin@rocket`).*

## Headline

The `siege` suite (renamed this session from `tadmor` = `kludge-tadmor` +
`tadmor` crucible fixture) charged on Cygwin Docker Desktop and ran the full
adversarial sortie set: **45 passed, 1 failed (46 total)**, clean quench.
That is the heat's headline essentially achieved — theurge's security suite
runs, and 45 escape vectors are correctly contained, on a box we do not
provision.

The single failure is recorded below. It does not diminish the result, but it
is a genuine finding that needs adjudication, **not** dismissal.

## The one breach

```
FAILED: rbtdrc_sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack
BREACH: spoofed ACK to 192.0.46.9:80 got response —
        conntrack RELATED,ESTABLISHED not filtering stateless ACKs
IFRIT_VERDICT: FAIL BREACH ...
```

Sortie: `rbtdrc_sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack` →
`rbtdrc_invoke_ifrit(ctx, "conntrack-spoofed-ack", ...)` — an ifrit sortie
driven by the `rbtid` adjutant. (Full packet/source construction lives in the
sortie script; not yet read — see Next Steps.)

Sentry FORWARD chain at the time (from the captured `iptables-rules.txt`):

```
-P FORWARD DROP
-A FORWARD -m state --state RELATED,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT
-A FORWARD -j RBM-FORWARD
...
-A RBM-FORWARD -d 192.0.32.0/20 -i eth0 -j ACCEPT      # allowlisted CIDR
```

## The crucial nuance — do not over-read this as a clean conntrack failure

The target **`192.0.46.9` is inside `192.0.32.0/20`**, which is an
**allowlisted** destination in the FORWARD chain. So a packet to it is permitted
**by the dest-based allowlist rule**, independent of conntrack state. The
sortie's verdict attributes the breach purely to "conntrack RELATED,ESTABLISHED
not filtering stateless ACKs," but that attribution is **incomplete**: to an
allowed dest, the allowlist alone would let the packet through.

Two non-exclusive readings, both worth testing:

1. **Defense-in-depth expectation** — the sortie asserts that a *stateless*
   forged ACK should not elicit a response *even to an allowed host* (only a
   properly established connection should). The current rules are dest-based,
   not state-gated, for allowed CIDRs.
2. **`nf_conntrack_tcp_loose=1`** — the kernel default makes conntrack adopt a
   lone mid-stream ACK as ESTABLISHED, so the top `RELATED,ESTABLISHED` rule
   accepts it before the allowlist is even consulted.

## The open question (platform divergence vs latent gap)

Does this breach reproduce on **native Linux / WSL** (e.g. `cerebro`)?

- **If it PASSES on native Linux but FAILS on Cygwin DD-WSL2** → a **platform
  verdict-divergence**: the Docker Desktop WSL2 / LinuxKit network stack handles
  conntrack (or `nf_conntrack_tcp_loose`) differently than native netfilter.
  This is a direct hit on the consumer-credibility claim — "a consumer can
  qualify their own install on their own machine and **trust the verdict**" —
  because the same suite would render a different security verdict depending on
  the host's docker backend.
- **If it FAILS everywhere** → a **latent property of the current sentry rules**
  that the cygwin run merely surfaced first (this suite had never run on cygwin
  before). Then it is a real egress-hardening item independent of platform.

Either outcome matters; we cannot tell which from this single run.

## Next steps to adjudicate

1. Read the `conntrack-spoofed-ack` sortie script in `rbtid` — exact packet
   construction (is the source spoofed? is the response in-band or via a
   conntrack-created return path?) and the precise verdict logic.
2. Run `siege` (and/or the `tadmor` crucible fixture) on **`cerebro`** (native
   Linux) and on the **WSL** side of `rocket` — compare the
   `conntrack_spoofed_ack` verdict across backends.
3. Compare `nf_conntrack_tcp_loose` (and related conntrack sysctls) between the
   DD-WSL2 kernel and native Linux.
4. If a real gap: harden the sentry egress — e.g. set `nf_conntrack_tcp_loose=0`,
   add `-m conntrack --ctstate INVALID -j DROP`, and/or state-gate the allowlist
   so allowed CIDRs still require an established connection.

## Provenance

- Run trace (cygwin): `temp-buk/temp-20260603-050730-21760-358/rbtd/`
  (`rbtdrc_sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack/{trace.txt,bark-stdout.txt}`,
  `rbtdrc_sentry_iptables_loaded/iptables-rules.txt`). Temp dirs are
  operator-mutable and may be reaped; the salient lines are quoted above.
- The bottle/crucible only charged on cygwin at all because of the two
  Windows-Docker-Desktop membranes — see
  `memo-20260603-windows-docker-desktop-bind-mount.md`.

## Addendum (2026-06-03, after blockade)

blockade (moriah — conjure-mode, GAR-summoned, airgap-built) ran on Cygwin and
produced the **identical 45/46** — the same single `conntrack_spoofed_ack`
BREACH. moriah carries a runtime egress allowlist too (`tcp443_allow_example`
and `cidr_all_ports_allowed` both passed), so the breach reproduces
**cross-nameplate** on Cygwin DD — it is *not* asymmetric between the tether and
airgap nameplates. That narrows the cause toward the shared sentry-rule /
conntrack property and makes the **native-Linux/WSL reproduction the decisive
next test**: does it breach there too?

content.text:
I now have the full mechanism in hand. Let me give you the mount orientation, then begin.

---

## Mounted: ₢BVAAF — conntrack-spoofed-ack-adjudication

**Goal in one sentence:** Determine whether the lone `conntrack_spoofed_ack` BREACH seen on Cygwin Docker Desktop is a *platform verdict-divergence* (Cygwin DD's WSL2/LinuxKit netfilter behaving differently from native Linux) or a *latent egress gap* in the sentry rules — by running siege on WSL first, then Cygwin DD, and comparing — then either hardening the sentry (if real gap) or documenting the divergence (if not).

**Heat context:** ₣BV (rbk-10-mvp-uncontrolled-cygwin-test) — the consumer-credibility claim that theurge can render a *trustworthy* security verdict on an unprovisioned box. siege ran 45/46 on Cygwin; this one breach is the only blemish, and the divergence-vs-gap question is a direct hit on "trust the verdict" if it's platform-dependent.

**What the code actually does (now confirmed at source, `rbida_sorties.rs:2891`):**
- Sends a bare TCP **ACK with no prior SYN** (src = bottle IP, src port 40080 → dst port 80) to an **allowed-CIDR** IP (`getent` of the connectivity domain → lands in `192.0.32.0/20`).
- Listens on a raw TCP socket; **any** TCP packet ≥20 bytes returning from that dst ⇒ `Ok(true)` ⇒ **BREACH**. Timeout ⇒ SECURE.
- The returning packet is a **RST** from the allowed host (it has no matching socket). The breach therefore requires *both* the outbound ACK to egress (allowlist permits it, dest-based) *and* the return RST to be admitted — which needs a conntrack entry. With `nf_conntrack_tcp_loose=1`, the lone ACK is adopted as ESTABLISHED, so the top `-m state RELATED,ESTABLISHED ACCEPT` admits the RST. That's the exact mechanism the docket names.

**Approach (4 steps):**
- **Reconfirm the split:** force WSL fundus → branch tip, run siege, capture the `conntrack_spoofed_ack` verdict. Then same on Cygwin DD. Keep the branch single-lined (rebase the fundus kludge-notch back to curia, push, before the next force-sync).
- **Adjudicate:** clean split (WSL SECURE / Cygwin BREACH) ⇒ divergence; breach on both ⇒ real gap. Corroborate with `nf_conntrack_tcp_loose` on each kernel.
- **If real gap:** harden sentry egress (tcp_loose=0, drop INVALID, or state-gate the allowlist) and re-test green on both.
- **If divergence:** document with rationale, reconcile the suite verdict across backends.

**Execution strategy:** Sequential, opus, single-operator. Remote work serialized (never parallel fixtures). I'll keep the curia ↔ fundus git line single before each run.

**Name assessment:** `conntrack-spoofed-ack-adjudication` fits the docket well — no rename needed.

On WSL the conntrack case **passes (SECURE)** — exactly the half of the split you predicted. Everything above it is green too (kludge-tadmor standalone, charge, all the pentacle/DNS/sentry/sortie cases). The suite is still running through the last few sorties (route-manipulation, enclave-subnet-escape at line 67), so I don't have the final tally line yet — but the one case the whole adjudication hinges on has landed green on WSL.

I won't peek again. I'll report the full WSL tally + clean quench when the background run notifies completion, then force-sync Cygwin and run the second half — where we expect that same case to be the lone failure.

echo "RBJp1: Beginning IPTables initialization"

echo "RBJp1: Set ephemeral port range for uplink connections"
echo "${RBRN_UPLINK_PORT_MIN} 65535" > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_local_port_range || exit 10

echo "RBJp1: Flushing existing rules"
iptables -F        || exit 10
iptables -t nat -F || exit 10

echo "RBJp1: Setting default policies"
iptables -P INPUT   DROP || exit 10
iptables -P FORWARD DROP || exit 10
iptables -P OUTPUT  DROP || exit 10

echo "RBJp1: Configuring loopback access"
iptables -A INPUT  -i lo -j ACCEPT || exit 10
iptables -A OUTPUT -o lo -j ACCEPT || exit 10

echo "RBJp1: Setting up connection tracking"
iptables -A INPUT   -m state --state RELATED,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT || exit 10
iptables -A FORWARD -m state --state RELATED,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT || exit 10
iptables -A OUTPUT  -m state --state RELATED,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT || exit 10

echo "RBJp1: Creating RBM chains"
iptables -N RBM-INGRESS || exit 10
iptables -N RBM-EGRESS  || exit 10
iptables -N RBM-FORWARD || exit 10

echo "RBJp1: Setting up chain jumps"
iptables -A INPUT   -j RBM-INGRESS || exit 10
iptables -A OUTPUT  -j RBM-EGRESS  || exit 10
iptables -A FORWARD -j RBM-FORWARD || exit 10

echo "RBJp2: Allowing ICMP within enclave only"
iptables -A RBM-INGRESS -i ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -p icmp -j ACCEPT || exit 20
iptables -A RBM-EGRESS  -o ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -p icmp -j ACCEPT || exit 20

echo "RBJp2: Enabling IP forwarding (required whenever sentry forwards on behalf of the enclave)"
echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward || exit 25

if test "${RBRN_ENTRY_MODE}" = "rbnne_enabled"; then
  echo "RBJp2c: Relaxing rp_filter to loose mode (required for interface-agnostic ingress)"
  # rp_filter=1 (strict) requires the packet's source-IP reverse-path to match the
  # ingress interface. Inbound published-port traffic from the framework gateway
  # (e.g., Docker Desktop 192.168.65.1) arrives on whichever interface Docker chose
  # for port-publish delivery, but its reverse route is always via the default
  # gateway on uplink — strict mode mismatches and drops at routing time, before
  # the iptables FORWARD chain ever sees the packet. Loose mode (rp_filter=2)
  # allows any source IP that's reachable via any interface, which is the actual
  # invariant for entry-port traffic. Iptables rules below still gate authorization;
  # rp_filter is not the load-bearing security control.
  echo 2 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/all/rp_filter || exit 25

  echo "RBJp2c: Configuring entry-port DNAT + MASQUERADE + FORWARD-ACCEPT"
  # Classification: destination port + per-IP source exclusion of the two enclave-internal
  # containers (sentry and bottle/pentacle). RETURN short-circuit pattern — if source matches
  # either enclave container IP, skip the DNAT; otherwise the unconditional DNAT below fires.
  #
  # Why per-IP and not whole-CIDR (! -s ENCLAVE_CIDR): on linux Docker Engine the host
  # attaches to the enclave bridge as a peer with the bridge gateway IP (.1), which is inside
  # the enclave CIDR by Docker convention. Whole-CIDR exclusion rejects every legitimate host
  # SYN (empirically confirmed on cerebro: PREROUTING DNAT fired 0 times). Per-IP excludes
  # exactly the threat surface — the two enclave container IPs known to compose — and lets
  # the bridge gateway, Desktop's VM gateway (192.168.65.1), rootlesskit's synthetic source
  # (10.0.2.100), and external clients all pass.
  #
  # Invariant defended: "enclave-internal sources MUST NOT reach sentry's entry port via DNAT"
  # (ifrit's direct_sentry_probe and net_dnat_entry_reflection sorties).
  iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -p tcp --dport "${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}" \
           -s "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP}" -j RETURN || exit 25
  iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -p tcp --dport "${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}" \
           -s "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP}" -j RETURN || exit 25
  iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -p tcp --dport "${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}" \
           -j DNAT --to-destination "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP}:${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE}" || exit 25

  # Return-path symmetry: MASQUERADE rewrites the source of the post-DNAT packet to
  # sentry's enclave IP, so the bottle's reply destination is in-enclave. The bottle then
  # routes via its directly-connected enclave route back to sentry, where conntrack reverses
  # the NAT cleanly. Without this, the bottle would reply to the original (possibly
  # framework-rewritten) source IP, traversing default-via-sentry into transit — empirically
  # broken on Docker Desktop per the topology-reframe scry diagnostic.
  iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -o ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -p tcp \
           -d "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP}" --dport "${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE}" -j MASQUERADE || exit 25

  # Authorization: only flows that sentry's own PREROUTING DNAT created conntrack state
  # for are allowed to forward. Conntrack DNAT-state is unforgeable from either bridge.
  # The parent FORWARD chain's ESTABLISHED,RELATED ACCEPT (line above) handles the return
  # path before reaching RBM-FORWARD.
  iptables -A RBM-FORWARD -p tcp \
           -d "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP}" --dport "${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE}" \
           -m conntrack --ctstate DNAT -j ACCEPT || exit 25
fi

echo "RBJp2b: Blocking ICMP cross-boundary traffic"
iptables -A RBM-FORWARD         -p icmp -j DROP || exit 28
iptables -A RBM-EGRESS  -o ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} -p icmp -j DROP || exit 28

echo "RBJp3: Phase 3: Access Setup"
if test "${RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE}" = "rbnne_disabled"; then
  echo "RBJp3: Blocking all non-port traffic"
  iptables -A RBM-EGRESS  -o ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} -j DROP || exit 30
  iptables -A RBM-FORWARD -i ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -j DROP || exit 30
else
  echo "RBJp3: Setting up uplink network hardening"
  echo 0 > /proc/sys/net/ipv6/conf/all/disable_ipv6    || exit 31
  echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/${RBJ_UPLINK_IF}/route_localnet || exit 31

  echo "RBJp3: Configuring NAT"
  iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -o ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} -s "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BASE_IP}/${RBRN_ENCLAVE_NETMASK}" \
                                       ! -d "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BASE_IP}/${RBRN_ENCLAVE_NETMASK}" \

content.text:
Crucial finding from the sentry source: it sets **no** conntrack sysctl (`tcp_loose`/`tcp_be_liberal` — none), and there is **no `ctstate INVALID -j DROP`** rule. So the sentry netns runs on the kernel *default* `nf_conntrack_tcp_loose`, and the entire breach hinges on whether the return RST is classified ESTABLISHED. The FORWARD order is: `RELATED,ESTABLISHED ACCEPT` first (line 100), then the dest-based allowlist in RBM-FORWARD. That confirms the docket's mechanism — now I measure the *actual* `tcp_loose` value in each sentry netns, because that's the lever that flips it.

Each host keeps its own kludge images + stamped digests, so I charge tadmor in place on each (WSL at `bb1f628f9`, Cygwin at `fa2175081`). **Charging tadmor on WSL first:**

content.text:
All three exited 255 simultaneously ~15s after the charge session closed — the sentry log shows it completed setup and "entering hold," so it was healthy. This is almost certainly **WSL2 distro auto-termination when the ssh session that charged it closed** (dockerd goes down with the distro → all containers exit 255 at once). The siege fixture never hits this because it charges→tests→quenches inside one process.

Fix: charge, probe, and quench **within a single ssh session** so the sentry is live during the probe. Running that chained on WSL:

input.command: ssh -o ConnectTimeout=15 -o BatchMode=yes -o ServerAliveInterval=30 wsl@rocket 'cd "$HOME/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster" && ./tt/rbw-cC.Charge.tadmor.sh >/tmp/ch.log 2>&1; echo "CHARGE_RC=$?"; S=canrbhm-tadmor-sentry; echo "running=$(docker inspect -f {{.State.Running}} $S 2>&1)"; echo "kernel=$(docker exec $S uname -r 2>&1)"; echo "tcp_loose=$(docker exec $S cat /proc/sys/net/netfilter/nf_conntrack_tcp_loose 2>&1)"; echo "be_liberal=$(docker exec $S cat /proc/sys/net/netfilter/nf_conntrack_tcp_be_liberal 2>&1)"; echo "--- FORWARD ---"; docker exec $S iptables -S FORWARD 2>&1; echo "--- RBM-FORWARD ---"; docker exec $S iptables -S RBM-FORWARD 2>&1; ./tt/rbw-cQ.Quench.tadmor.sh >/tmp/qu.log 2>&1; echo "QUENCH_RC=$?"'

content.text:
**WSL sentry profile captured** — and it overturns the simple theory:

- kernel: `5.15.167.4-microsoft-standard-WSL2`
- **`nf_conntrack_tcp_loose = 1`** ← and yet WSL **passes** (no RST returns)
- `tcp_be_liberal = 0`
- FORWARD: DROP policy, `RELATED,ESTABLISHED` accept, then RBM-FORWARD with the `-d 192.0.32.0/20 -i eth0 -j ACCEPT` allowlist. No INVALID drop — exactly the source.

So `tcp_loose=1` **alone does not cause the breach** — WSL has it set to 1 and is still SECURE. The divergence must therefore be in how the *DD kernel* actually tracks the lone-ACK flow (or in DD's NAT/network stack), not in this sysctl. The decisive comparison is the Cygwin DD sentry's kernel + same values. Probing it now (chained, same method):

input.command: ssh -o ConnectTimeout=15 -o BatchMode=yes -o ServerAliveInterval=30 cygwin@rocket 'cd "$HOME/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster" && ./tt/rbw-cC.Charge.tadmor.sh >/tmp/ch.log 2>&1; echo "CHARGE_RC=$?"; S=canrbhm-tadmor-sentry; echo "running=$(docker inspect -f {{.State.Running}} $S 2>&1)"; echo "kernel=$(docker exec $S uname -r 2>&1)"; echo "tcp_loose=$(docker exec $S cat /proc/sys/net/netfilter/nf_conntrack_tcp_loose 2>&1)"; echo "be_liberal=$(docker exec $S cat /proc/sys/net/netfilter/nf_conntrack_tcp_be_liberal 2>&1)"; echo "--- FORWARD ---"; docker exec $S iptables -S FORWARD 2>&1; echo "--- RBM-FORWARD ---"; docker exec $S iptables -S RBM-FORWARD 2>&1; ./tt/rbw-cQ.Quench.tadmor.sh >/tmp/qu.log 2>&1; echo "QUENCH_RC=$?"'

content.text:
This is the decisive static result: **the two backends are byte-identical on every dimension that could explain the breach** —

| Dimension | WSL (native docker) | Cygwin (Docker Desktop) |
|---|---|---|
| kernel | `5.15.167.4-microsoft-standard-WSL2` | **same** |
| `nf_conntrack_tcp_loose` | `1` | **`1`** |
| `tcp_be_liberal` | `0` | **`0`** |
| FORWARD + RBM-FORWARD rules | (full allowlist) | **byte-identical** |
| `conntrack_spoofed_ack` verdict | **SECURE** | **BREACH** |

Same kernel, same conntrack config, same iptables — opposite verdict. That **rules out the sentry's egress posture as the cause** (it's identical and correct on WSL). The only remaining variable is the docker *network substrate*: native kernel bridge/NAT vs Docker Desktop's userspace gateway (gvisor-tap-vsock). My hypothesis: **DD's userspace gateway itself responds to the out-of-state ACK** (reflects/synthesizes a RST appearing to come from the dst), which real kernel egress on WSL does not.

To confirm with direct observation, I need a packet capture during the repro. Let me nail the exact case-name and check whether the sentry image even has tcpdump — locally, before spending remote round-trips:

input.command: echo "=== exact FixtureCase case-name for conntrack (how cases register) ==="; grep -rn 'conntrack[-_]spoofed' Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/ | grep -iv 'fn sortie' | head; echo; echo "=== case-name mapping (registry / dispatch) ==="; grep -rn 'conntrack' Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrc_crucible.rs | head; echo; echo "=== tcpdump / conntrack in sentry image? ==="; grep -in 'tcpdump\|conntrack\|iproute\|net-tools\|apk add\|apt-get install\|RUN ' rbmm_moorings/rbmv_vessels/common-sentry-context/Dockerfile | head -40

content.text:
Both confirmed: case registers as `rbtdrc_sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack`, and the sentry image ships **tcpdump + iproute2** (no conntrack CLI, but I can read `/proc/net/nf_conntrack` directly). 

Now the smoking-gun experiment: charge, fire **just** the conntrack sortie via `rbw-tc`, and capture on the sentry with tcpdump to see **what returns from 192.0.46.9 and on which leg**. Running it on the **breaching side (Cygwin DD) first** to characterize the response:

input.command: ssh -o ConnectTimeout=15 -o BatchMode=yes -o ServerAliveInterval=30 cygwin@rocket 'cd "$HOME/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster" && ./tt/rbw-cC.Charge.tadmor.sh >/tmp/ch.log 2>&1; echo "CHARGE_RC=$?"; S=canrbhm-tadmor-sentry; echo "=== sentry interfaces ==="; docker exec $S ip -o -4 addr 2>&1; ( sleep 3; ./tt/rbw-tc.FixtureCase.sh tadmor rbtdrc_sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack >/tmp/case.log 2>&1 ) & echo "=== tcpdump (any iface, host 192.0.46.9, 12s; case fires ~3s) ==="; docker exec $S sh -c "timeout 12 tcpdump -i any -nn -v -l tcp and host 192.0.46.9" 2>/tmp/td.err; echo "--- tcpdump stderr ---"; tail -2 /tmp/td.err; echo "=== conntrack (procfs) ==="; docker exec $S sh -c "grep 192.0.46.9 /proc/net/nf_conntrack || echo NO-CT-ENTRY"; echo "=== case result ==="; tail -8 /tmp/case.log; ./tt/rbw-cQ.Quench.tadmor.sh >/tmp/qu.log 2>&1; echo "QUENCH_RC=$?"'

content.text:
Heeded — and your nudge is well-timed, because my hand-rolled capture just produced a paradox that scry is the right tool to resolve. Look at what the sentry tcpdump showed:

```
eth1 In   10.242.0.3.40080 > 192.0.46.9.80: Flags [.]  (the bottle's lone ACK arriving)
eth0 Out  10.242.0.3.40080 > 192.0.46.9.80: Flags [.]  (forwarded out to uplink)
--- 2 packets received by filter ---
```

**The ACK egressed, but NO response from 192.0.46.9 ever came back through the sentry** — yet the bottle's raw socket still reported a response (BREACH). So whatever the bottle "heard" did **not traverse the sentry uplink**. My sentry-only tcpdump is blind to it. That's exactly why scry — which is built to observe the crucible's *containers* (including the bottle) — is the right instrument here, not my improvised sentry capture.

Let me read how scry is driven so I can run it non-interactively against the bottle:

input.command: echo "=== scry routing + impl ==="; grep -rn 'scry\|Scry\|rbw-cs' Tools/rbk/*.sh Tools/rbk/rbtd/ 2>/dev/null | grep -iv 'rbk-claude' | head -30

rbob_scry() {
  zrbob_sentinel

  buc_doc_brief "Scry network traffic on crucible containers"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  # Kindle observe module and delegate
  zrboo_kindle
  rboo_observe
}

rbob_charged() {
  zrbob_sentinel

  buc_doc_brief "Check whether the crucible is charged (compose project has running containers)"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  rbob_charged_predicate
}

######################################################################
# Furnish and Main

zrbob_furnish() {
  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env "BUZ_FOLIO             " "Nameplate moniker (e.g., tadmor)"
  buc_doc_env_done || return 0

  local z_command="${1:-}"

  local z_rbk_kit_dir="${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/burd_regime.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbrn_regime.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbcc_Constants.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbrr_regime.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbrd_regime.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbdc_DerivedConstants.sh"
  source "${RBCC_rbrr_file}"
  source "${RBCC_rbrd_file}"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbgc_Constants.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbgl_GarLayout.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbgd_DepotConstants.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbgo_OAuth.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbob_bottle.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buf_fact.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbfh_FoundryHygiene.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbfk_kludge.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rboo_observe.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buz_zipper.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbz_zipper.sh"

  zbuv_kindle
  zburd_kindle
  zrbcc_kindle

  local z_folio="${BUZ_FOLIO:-}"
  if test -z "${z_folio}"; then
    local z_monikers
    z_monikers=$(rbrn_list_capture) || buc_die "No nameplates found"
    buc_step "Available nameplates:"
    local z_moniker=""
    for z_moniker in ${z_monikers}; do
      buc_bare "        ${z_moniker}"
    done
    buc_die "Nameplate moniker required (pass as argument)"
  fi
  local z_nameplate_file="${RBCC_moorings_dir}/${z_folio}/${RBCC_rbrn_file}"
  test -f "${z_nameplate_file}" || buc_die "Nameplate not found: ${z_nameplate_file}"
  source "${z_nameplate_file}" || buc_die "Failed to source nameplate: ${z_nameplate_file}"
  zrbrn_kindle

  # Differential enforce: nameplate-keyed kludge commands write
  # RBRN_*_HALLMARK, so zrbrn_enforce would self-veto on marshal-zero

18	#
19	# RBOO - Recipe Bottle Orchestration Observe
20	# Network observation (tcpdump) for crucibles
21	#
22	# Requires: buc_command.sh sourced
23	# Requires: rbob_bottle.sh sourced and kindled
24	
25	set -euo pipefail
26	
27	# Multiple inclusion detection
28	test -z "${ZRBOO_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module rboo multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
29	ZRBOO_SOURCED=1
30	
31	######################################################################
32	# Kindle and Sentinel
33	
34	zrboo_kindle() {
35	  test -z "${ZRBOO_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module rboo already kindled"
36	
37	  # Verify RBOB is kindled (provides container names, runtime)
38	  zrbob_sentinel
39	
40	  # Terminal control sequences for colored output (ANSI literals, matching BUC constants)
41	  readonly ZRBOO_BOLD=$'\033[1m'
42	  readonly ZRBOO_YELLOW=$'\033[33m'
43	  readonly ZRBOO_BLUE=$'\033[34m'
44	  readonly ZRBOO_WHITE=$'\033[37m'
45	  readonly ZRBOO_RESET=$'\033[0m'
46	
47	  # Common tcpdump options: unbuffered, line-buffered, no name resolution, verbose
48	  ZRBOO_TCPDUMP_OPTS=(-U -l -nn -vvv)
49	  readonly ZRBOO_TCPDUMP_OPTS
50	
51	  # Bridge interface (only for podman, discovered at observe time)
52	  z_rboo_bridge_interface=""
53	
54	  readonly ZRBOO_KINDLED=1
55	}
56	
57	zrboo_sentinel() {
58	  test "${ZRBOO_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rboo not kindled - call zrboo_kindle first"
59	}
60	
61	######################################################################
62	# Output Prefixing Functions
63	
64	zrboo_prefix_pentacle() {
65	  while IFS= read -r line; do
66	    echo "${ZRBOO_YELLOW}${ZRBOO_BOLD}[PENTACLE/BOTTLE]${ZRBOO_RESET} ${line}"
67	  done
68	}
69	
70	zrboo_prefix_sentry() {
71	  while IFS= read -r line; do
72	    echo "${ZRBOO_WHITE}${ZRBOO_BOLD}[SENTRY]${ZRBOO_RESET} ${line}"
73	  done
74	}
75	
76	zrboo_prefix_bridge() {
77	  while IFS= read -r line; do
78	    echo "${ZRBOO_BLUE}${ZRBOO_BOLD}[BRIDGE]${ZRBOO_RESET} ${line}"
79	  done
80	}
81	
82	######################################################################
83	# Public API
84	
85	# Observe network traffic on crucible containers
86	# Runs until Ctrl+C; all captures run in parallel
87	rboo_observe() {
88	  zrboo_sentinel
89	
90	  buc_step "Starting network observation: ${RBRN_MONIKER}"
91	
92	  # Set up cleanup trap
93	  trap 'buc_info "Stopping captures..."; kill 0 2>/dev/null; exit 0' SIGINT SIGTERM
94	
95	  buc_info "Network topology:"
96	  buc_info "  SENTRY: Bridge (eth0) <-> Internet, Enclave (eth1) <-> Internal"
97	  buc_info "  PENTACLE/BOTTLE: Shared namespace on Enclave network (eth0)"
98	
99	  # Start pentacle capture (shared namespace with bottle)
100	  buc_info "Starting pentacle/bottle capture (eth0)"
101	  "${ZRBOB_RUNTIME}" exec "${ZRBOB_PENTACLE}" tcpdump "${ZRBOO_TCPDUMP_OPTS[@]}" -i eth0 2>&1 | zrboo_prefix_pentacle &
102	
103	  # Start sentry enclave capture
104	  buc_info "Starting sentry enclave capture (eth1)"
105	  "${ZRBOB_RUNTIME}" exec "${ZRBOB_SENTRY}" tcpdump "${ZRBOO_TCPDUMP_OPTS[@]}" -i eth1 2>&1 | zrboo_prefix_sentry &
106	
107	  # Bridge capture: only for podman (requires podman machine ssh)
108	  if test "${RBRN_RUNTIME}" = "podman"; then
109	    # Discover bridge interface for enclave network
110	    z_rboo_bridge_interface=$(${ZRBOB_RUNTIME} network inspect "${ZRBOB_NETWORK}" --format '{{.NetworkInterface}}')
111	    buc_info "Starting bridge capture (${z_rboo_bridge_interface}) via podman machine ssh"
112	    ${ZRBOB_RUNTIME} machine ssh "sudo -n tcpdump ${ZRBOO_TCPDUMP_OPTS} -i ${z_rboo_bridge_interface}" 2>&1 | zrboo_prefix_bridge &
113	  else
114	    buc_info "Bridge capture not available for Docker runtime (requires podman machine ssh)"
115	  fi
116	
117	  buc_info "Press Ctrl+C to stop captures"
118	
119	  # Wait for all background processes
120	  wait
121	}
122	
123	# eof

#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2025 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# RBOO - Recipe Bottle Orchestration Observe
# Network observation (tcpdump) for crucibles
#
# Requires: buc_command.sh sourced
# Requires: rbob_bottle.sh sourced and kindled

set -euo pipefail

# Multiple inclusion detection
test -z "${ZRBOO_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module rboo multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
ZRBOO_SOURCED=1

######################################################################
# Kindle and Sentinel

zrboo_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZRBOO_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module rboo already kindled"

  # Verify RBOB is kindled (provides container names, runtime)
  zrbob_sentinel

  # Terminal control sequences for colored output (ANSI literals, matching BUC constants)
  readonly ZRBOO_BOLD=$'\033[1m'
  readonly ZRBOO_YELLOW=$'\033[33m'
  readonly ZRBOO_BLUE=$'\033[34m'
  readonly ZRBOO_WHITE=$'\033[37m'
  readonly ZRBOO_RESET=$'\033[0m'

  # Common tcpdump options: unbuffered, line-buffered, no name resolution, verbose
  ZRBOO_TCPDUMP_OPTS=(-U -l -nn -vvv)
  readonly ZRBOO_TCPDUMP_OPTS

  # Bridge interface (only for podman, discovered at observe time)
  z_rboo_bridge_interface=""

  readonly ZRBOO_KINDLED=1
}

zrboo_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBOO_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rboo not kindled - call zrboo_kindle first"
}

######################################################################
# Output Prefixing Functions

zrboo_prefix_pentacle() {
  while IFS= read -r line; do
    echo "${ZRBOO_YELLOW}${ZRBOO_BOLD}[PENTACLE/BOTTLE]${ZRBOO_RESET} ${line}"
  done
}

zrboo_prefix_sentry() {
  while IFS= read -r line; do
    echo "${ZRBOO_WHITE}${ZRBOO_BOLD}[SENTRY]${ZRBOO_RESET} ${line}"
  done
}

zrboo_prefix_bridge() {
  while IFS= read -r line; do
    echo "${ZRBOO_BLUE}${ZRBOO_BOLD}[BRIDGE]${ZRBOO_RESET} ${line}"
  done
}

######################################################################
# Public API

# Observe network traffic on crucible containers
# Runs until Ctrl+C; all captures run in parallel
rboo_observe() {
  zrboo_sentinel

  buc_step "Starting network observation: ${RBRN_MONIKER}"

  # Set up cleanup trap
  trap 'buc_info "Stopping captures..."; kill 0 2>/dev/null; exit 0' SIGINT SIGTERM

  buc_info "Network topology:"
  buc_info "  SENTRY: Bridge (eth0) <-> Internet, Enclave (eth1) <-> Internal"
  buc_info "  PENTACLE/BOTTLE: Shared namespace on Enclave network (eth0)"

  # Start pentacle capture (shared namespace with bottle)
  buc_info "Starting pentacle/bottle capture (eth0)"
  "${ZRBOB_RUNTIME}" exec "${ZRBOB_PENTACLE}" tcpdump "${ZRBOO_TCPDUMP_OPTS[@]}" -i eth0 2>&1 | zrboo_prefix_pentacle &

  # Start sentry enclave capture
  buc_info "Starting sentry enclave capture (eth1)"
  "${ZRBOB_RUNTIME}" exec "${ZRBOB_SENTRY}" tcpdump "${ZRBOO_TCPDUMP_OPTS[@]}" -i eth1 2>&1 | zrboo_prefix_sentry &

  # Bridge capture: only for podman (requires podman machine ssh)
  if test "${RBRN_RUNTIME}" = "podman"; then
    # Discover bridge interface for enclave network
    z_rboo_bridge_interface=$(${ZRBOB_RUNTIME} network inspect "${ZRBOB_NETWORK}" --format '{{.NetworkInterface}}')
    buc_info "Starting bridge capture (${z_rboo_bridge_interface}) via podman machine ssh"
    ${ZRBOB_RUNTIME} machine ssh "sudo -n tcpdump ${ZRBOO_TCPDUMP_OPTS} -i ${z_rboo_bridge_interface}" 2>&1 | zrboo_prefix_bridge &
  else
    buc_info "Bridge capture not available for Docker runtime (requires podman machine ssh)"
  fi

  buc_info "Press Ctrl+C to stop captures"

  # Wait for all background processes
  wait
}

# eof

content.text:
Now the topology is clear from scry's own code (lines 95-97) — and it exposes my blind spot:

- **SENTRY**: eth0 = bridge/uplink ↔ Internet, eth1 = enclave ↔ internal
- **PENTACLE/BOTTLE**: *share a netns*; their enclave interface is **eth0**

scry captures the **pentacle/bottle eth0** and **sentry eth1** in parallel — and I only ever looked at the sentry. The bottle's view is exactly where the "response" must appear to trigger BREACH. So I'll apply scry's capture points (pentacle/bottle + sentry enclave), plus the uplink, with MAC addresses (`-e`) so I can tell **whether any response crossed the sentry boundary or was injected straight onto the enclave bridge by DD**. Driving it non-interactively on Cygwin DD:

input.command: ssh -o ConnectTimeout=15 -o BatchMode=yes -o ServerAliveInterval=30 cygwin@rocket 'cd "$HOME/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster" && ./tt/rbw-cC.Charge.tadmor.sh >/tmp/ch.log 2>&1; echo CHARGE_RC=$?; S=canrbhm-tadmor-sentry; P=canrbhm-tadmor-pentacle; echo "=== MACs/addrs ==="; echo "[sentry]"; docker exec $S ip -o link 2>&1 | grep -iE "eth|ether"; docker exec $S ip -o -4 addr 2>&1 | grep eth; echo "[pentacle/bottle]"; docker exec $P ip -o link 2>&1 | grep -iE "eth|ether"; docker exec $P ip -o -4 addr 2>&1 | grep eth; docker exec -d $S sh -c "timeout 12 tcpdump -i eth0 -nn -e -vvv -l host 192.0.46.9 > /tmp/s0.txt 2>&1"; docker exec -d $S sh -c "timeout 12 tcpdump -i eth1 -nn -e -vvv -l host 192.0.46.9 > /tmp/s1.txt 2>&1"; ( sleep 3; ./tt/rbw-tc.FixtureCase.sh tadmor rbtdrc_sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack >/tmp/case.log 2>&1 ) & echo "=== PENTACLE/BOTTLE eth0 (foreground 12s) ==="; docker exec $P sh -c "timeout 12 tcpdump -i eth0 -nn -e -vvv -l host 192.0.46.9" 2>/tmp/pd.err; echo "--- pent err ---"; tail -1 /tmp/pd.err; echo "=== SENTRY eth0 UPLINK ==="; docker exec $S cat /tmp/s0.txt 2>&1; echo "=== SENTRY eth1 ENCLAVE ==="; docker exec $S cat /tmp/s1.txt 2>&1; echo "=== case result ==="; tail -6 /tmp/case.log; ./tt/rbw-cQ.Quench.tadmor.sh >/tmp/qu.log 2>&1; echo QUENCH_RC=$?'

content.text:
**This is the smoking gun.** Look at the MAC addresses on the bottle's own interface:

**Bottle/pentacle eth0 capture (Cygwin DD) — 2 packets:**
```
365271  16:76:99:27:5f:30 (bottle)         > 9e:1b:dd:ef:50:fb (sentry-enclave)  10.242.0.3.40080 > 192.0.46.9.80: Flags [.]   ← ACK out
365754  ce:c3:90:4a:af:1a (??? NOT sentry) > 16:76:99:27:5f:30 (bottle)          192.0.46.9.80 > 10.242.0.3.40080: Flags [R]   ← RST in
```

**Sentry uplink (eth0): only the outbound ACK. Sentry enclave (eth1): only the outbound ACK. No RST on either sentry leg.**

So the RST that breaches **never traversed the sentry at all**. It appears on the enclave bridge sourced from MAC `ce:c3:90:4a:af:1a` — which is **neither the sentry's enclave interface (`9e:1b…`) nor the bottle**. That's **Docker Desktop's userspace bridge (gvisor-tap-vsock) synthesizing the RST and injecting it directly onto the enclave segment**, off-path, bypassing the sentry's FORWARD chain. The sentry forwarded the ACK out correctly and would have governed any real return — but DD answered the spoofed ACK *on the bridge itself*. conntrack/`tcp_loose` never even enters into it; the packet the sortie "hears" was manufactured below the containment boundary.

That's a definitive **platform divergence**, not an egress gap. Now the clean mirror — same bottle-side capture on **WSL** (real kernel bridge) to confirm the bottle hears nothing there:

input.command: ssh -o ConnectTimeout=15 -o BatchMode=yes -o ServerAliveInterval=30 wsl@rocket 'cd "$HOME/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster" && ./tt/rbw-cC.Charge.tadmor.sh >/tmp/ch.log 2>&1; echo CHARGE_RC=$?; S=canrbhm-tadmor-sentry; P=canrbhm-tadmor-pentacle; echo "=== MACs/addrs ==="; echo "[sentry]"; docker exec $S ip -o link 2>&1 | grep -iE "eth|ether"; docker exec $S ip -o -4 addr 2>&1 | grep eth; echo "[pentacle/bottle]"; docker exec $P ip -o link 2>&1 | grep -iE "eth|ether"; docker exec $P ip -o -4 addr 2>&1 | grep eth; docker exec -d $S sh -c "timeout 12 tcpdump -i eth0 -nn -e -vvv -l host 192.0.46.9 > /tmp/s0.txt 2>&1"; docker exec -d $S sh -c "timeout 12 tcpdump -i eth1 -nn -e -vvv -l host 192.0.46.9 > /tmp/s1.txt 2>&1"; ( sleep 3; ./tt/rbw-tc.FixtureCase.sh tadmor rbtdrc_sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack >/tmp/case.log 2>&1 ) & echo "=== PENTACLE/BOTTLE eth0 (foreground 12s) ==="; docker exec $P sh -c "timeout 12 tcpdump -i eth0 -nn -e -vvv -l host 192.0.46.9" 2>/tmp/pd.err; echo "--- pent err ---"; tail -1 /tmp/pd.err; echo "=== SENTRY eth0 UPLINK ==="; docker exec $S cat /tmp/s0.txt 2>&1; echo "=== SENTRY eth1 ENCLAVE ==="; docker exec $S cat /tmp/s1.txt 2>&1; echo "=== case result ==="; tail -6 /tmp/case.log; ./tt/rbw-cQ.Quench.tadmor.sh >/tmp/qu.log 2>&1; echo QUENCH_RC=$?'

The WSL mirror seals it. Same experiment, real kernel bridge:

**Bottle/pentacle eth0 capture (WSL) — 1 packet only:**
```
660661  92:e0:9f:ba:72:48 (bottle) > ea:82:cb:9f:d4:99 (sentry)  10.242.0.3.40080 > 192.0.46.9.80: Flags [.]   ← ACK out
                              (nothing returns)
```
Sentry legs show the ACK ingressing from the bottle (ttl 64) and forwarded out the uplink (ttl 63). **No RST anywhere.** Case **PASSED (SECURE)**.

The cross-platform contrast is now airtight:

| | Bottle sends ACK | Sentry forwards out uplink | Response to bottle | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **WSL** (real kernel bridge) | ✓ | ✓ (ttl decremented) | **none** | SECURE |
| **Cygwin DD** (userspace bridge) | ✓ | ✓ (ttl decremented) | **RST injected on enclave bridge from `ce:c3:90:4a:af:1a` — a non-sentry MAC** | BREACH |

The verdict is **platform divergence**, and I can prove *mechanism*, not just correlation: on DD the breaching RST is delivered to the bottle from a host-side MAC that is neither the sentry nor the bottle, and it **never appears on either sentry interface** — so it bypassed the containment boundary entirely. Let me corroborate *why* DD does this (its userspace networking answering unknown TCP flows) with the websearch you pointed me at:

input.query: Docker Desktop WSL2 bridge network gateway responds RST to container TCP without SYN gvisor-tap-vsock

content.content:
Web search results for query: "Docker Desktop WSL2 bridge network gateway responds RST to container TCP without SYN gvisor-tap-vsock"

Links: [{"title":"GitHub - containers/gvisor-tap-vsock: A new network stack based on gVisor · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/containers/gvisor-tap-vsock"},{"title":"Createing a bridge network - Docker Desktop - Docker Community Forums","url":"https://forums.docker.com/t/createing-a-bridge-network/122471"},{"title":"gvproxy | containers/gvisor-tap-vsock | DeepWiki","url":"https://deepwiki.com/containers/gvisor-tap-vsock/2.1-gvproxy"},{"title":"WSL Mirrored Mode Networking in Docker Desktop 4.26.0 - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/docker/wsl-mirrored-mode-networking-in-docker-desktop-4260-4b86"},{"title":"Remote control of Docker Desktop on Windows with WSL2 - Docker Desktop - Docker Community Forums","url":"https://forums.docker.com/t/remote-control-of-docker-desktop-on-windows-with-wsl2/138998"},{"title":"How Docker Desktop Networking Works Under the Hood | Docker","url":"https://www.docker.com/blog/how-docker-desktop-networking-works-under-the-hood/"},{"title":"Use bridge networking instead of NAT in Docker Desktop on Windows - Docker Desktop - Docker Community Forums","url":"https://forums.docker.com/t/use-bridge-networking-instead-of-nat-in-docker-desktop-on-windows/147589"},{"title":"WSL2: Docker doesn't work with default bridged network · Issue #4133 · microsoft/WSL","url":"https://github.com/microsoft/WSL/issues/4133"}]

The search results returned information about Docker Desktop networking, WSL2, and the gvisor-tap-vsock project, but they don't contain specific information about the issue where the Docker Desktop WSL2 bridge network gateway responds with RST (TCP reset) packets to container TCP connections without SYN.

However, I can provide some relevant context from the results:

When a connection is rejected, vpnkit (Docker's networking component) replies with a TCP RST (reset) packet which causes the connect() inside Linux to return an error. This is standard behavior for rejected connections in Docker Desktop.

The search results show that gvisor-tap-vsock brings a configurable DNS server and dynamic port forwarding and can be used with QEMU, Hyperkit, Hyper-V and User Mode Linux, which is an alternative networking stack that could potentially be involved in WSL2 networking scenarios.

For more specific information about this particular networking issue (RST responses without SYN), you may want to check Docker's GitHub issues, the Docker Community Forums, or the gvisor-tap-vsock project's issue tracker, as this appears to be a very specific technical problem that may be documented in those venues.
