content.text:
The websearch corroborates the mechanism: Docker's userspace networking component (vpnkit historically, gvisor-tap-vsock in current Docker Desktop) **replies with a TCP RST when a connection is rejected/unknown** — exactly the off-path RST I captured. The bridge traffic on Windows DD traverses this userspace stack, while native docker uses real kernel bridges that have no such responder.

---

## Verdict: platform divergence — confirmed at packet level, not a sentry egress gap

**The `conntrack_spoofed_ack` "breach" on Cygwin is an artifact of Docker Desktop's userspace network emulation, not a containment failure.** The sentry's egress posture is correct and byte-identical on both platforms.

**Evidence chain, tightest first:**

1. **Everything the sentry controls is identical.** Same kernel (`5.15.167.4-microsoft-standard-WSL2`), same `nf_conntrack_tcp_loose=1`, same `tcp_be_liberal=0`, byte-identical FORWARD/RBM-FORWARD rules — yet WSL is SECURE and Cygwin BREACHES. So the cause is *outside* the sentry's configuration. (This also kills the original memo's leading hypothesis: `tcp_loose=1` is set on the **secure** platform too, so it isn't the lever.)

2. **The breaching RST never crosses the sentry.** On Cygwin, both sentry interfaces captured **only the outbound ACK** (ingress ttl 64 → uplink egress ttl 63). No RST on either leg. The sentry forwarded the ACK out (the dest is legitimately allowlisted) and would have governed any real return — but no return ever reached it.

3. **The RST is injected onto the enclave bridge from a non-sentry MAC.** On the bottle's own interface:
   ```
   ce:c3:90:4a:af:1a  >  16:76:99:27:5f:30 (bottle)   192.0.46.9.80 > 10.242.0.3.40080: Flags [R]
   ```
   `ce:c3:…` is neither the sentry's enclave MAC (`9e:1b:…`) nor the bottle — it's Docker Desktop's host-side enclave gateway. DD's userspace stack manufactured the RST and delivered it to the bottle **below the containment boundary**.

4. **WSL mirror: bottle hears nothing.** Identical experiment on the real kernel bridge — the bottle's interface shows only its own outgoing ACK, no response. SECURE.

**Why this is divergence and not a real gap** (stating the skeptical case honestly): the bottle *did* receive a TCP response to a spoofed ACK aimed at an allowlisted host — that looks alarming. But the response is an empty RST (`win 0`, no payload) **synthesized by DD's network emulation**, not a reply that traversed real egress and returned through the sentry. There is no data channel and no real return path — the sentry forwards to the allowlisted CIDR by design and saw no return to govern. The sortie tests a defense-in-depth property ("a stateless ACK shouldn't draw a response"); on DD that property *appears* violated purely because DD answers on the bridge. It's a divergence in **test observability**, not in **security posture**. The other 45 sorties — which test real reachability with payloads — pass identically on both.

## What the docket's "Done" still needs (your call — I'm not changing code unprompted)

The divergence branch of Done is: *"documented with rationale and the suite verdict reconciled across backends."* Two pieces:

1. **Document** — update `memo-20260603-cygwin-ifrit-first-run-conntrack.md` with this resolution (the packet-level proof + MAC evidence + mechanism). Low-risk, squarely the "document with rationale" item. I'd recommend doing it.
2. **Reconcile the verdict across backends** — a design decision for the sortie on Docker Desktop. Options:
   - **(a)** Mark `conntrack_spoofed_ack` xfail/skip on DD backends with a documented rationale (cleanest; preserves the verdict's meaning — "trust the verdict" holds because the suite *knows* this case is platform-blind on DD).
   - **(b)** Harden the sortie to validate the response actually transited the sentry (e.g., reject responses whose L2 source isn't the sentry gateway) — makes it robust, more code.
   - **(c)** Leave as-is, documented only — but then DD users see a red breach that isn't one, denting the "trust the verdict" claim.

   This touches a security test's semantics, so I'd rather surface it than pick unilaterally. My lean: **(a)** now (correctness of the verdict-trust claim), with (b) noted as the more thorough follow-up.

**Housekeeping:** both crucibles quenched cleanly; charges didn't kludge so no new stamps. Branch state unchanged — curia/origin/WSL at `bb1f628f9`, Cygwin's 2 host-local stamps still unpushed (harmless).

How do you want to proceed — shall I write up the memo resolution, and which reconciliation option (a/b/c)?

**Sources:**
- [How Docker Desktop Networking Works Under the Hood — Docker](https://www.docker.com/blog/how-docker-desktop-networking-works-under-the-hood/)
- [containers/gvisor-tap-vsock — GitHub](https://github.com/containers/gvisor-tap-vsock)
- [gvproxy — gvisor-tap-vsock — DeepWiki](https://deepwiki.com/containers/gvisor-tap-vsock/2.1-gvproxy)
- [WSL Mirrored Mode Networking in Docker Desktop 4.26.0 — DEV](https://dev.to/docker/wsl-mirrored-mode-networking-in-docker-desktop-4260-4b86)

content.text: I'll write a detailed, durable memo capturing the full adjudication with the packet-level evidence, then we'll talk repair.

input.content:
# conntrack_spoofed_ack adjudicated: Docker Desktop network-emulation artifact, not a sentry egress gap

*2026-06-04. Heat ₣BV (rbk-10-mvp-uncontrolled-cygwin-test), pace ₢BVAAF
(conntrack-spoofed-ack-adjudication). Resolves the open question left by
`memo-20260603-cygwin-ifrit-first-run-conntrack.md`.*

## Verdict

The lone `conntrack_spoofed_ack` BREACH observed on uncontrolled Cygwin + Docker
Desktop is a **platform verdict-divergence**, proven at packet level. It is **not**
a latent egress gap in the sentry. The sentry's containment is correct and
byte-identical across backends; the "response" the sortie detects on Docker
Desktop is a TCP RST **manufactured by Docker Desktop's userspace network stack
and injected directly onto the enclave bridge, bypassing the sentry entirely.**
On native Linux (WSL2, real kernel bridge) no such response exists and the case
passes.

The original memo's leading hypothesis — `nf_conntrack_tcp_loose=1` letting a
lone mid-stream ACK be adopted as ESTABLISHED — is **disproven**: that sysctl is
`1` on the *secure* platform too (see static comparison below). conntrack state
never enters into it, because the breaching packet never traverses the sentry's
conntrack at all.

## How it was adjudicated

All work rode branch `bv-conntrack-20260604-BVAAF` (off the requal wrap
`80b681e62`). Both rocket funduses were attached directly to GitHub (WSL via SSH,
Cygwin via HTTPS) and force-synced to the branch tip before each run. WSL is
native Docker (`docker 29.1.3`); Cygwin is Docker Desktop (`docker 28.3.2`).

### 1. Recreate — clean cross-platform split

`siege` (= `kludge-tadmor` + `tadmor` crucible) run on each backend from the same
integral source tip:

| Backend | siege tally | `conntrack_spoofed_ack` |
|---------|-------------|-------------------------|
| WSL (native docker) | 60 passed / 0 failed | **PASSED (SECURE)** |
| Cygwin (Docker Desktop) | 45 passed / 1 failed | **FAILED (BREACH)** |

This reproduces the original memo's `45/46` exactly. Note the crucible fixture
**fail-fasts**: on Cygwin it halts at `conntrack_spoofed_ack` (case 46) and
quenches, so the 13 cases that run after it on WSL never execute on Cygwin. "45/46"
means "stopped at first failure," not "1 of the full 59 failed." Single-case
reruns via `rbw-tc tadmor rbtdrc_sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack` confirmed the split
independently (WSL PASS, Cygwin FAIL).

### 2. Static comparison — everything the sentry controls is identical

Measured inside the live `tadmor` sentry netns on each backend:

| Dimension | WSL | Cygwin DD |
|-----------|-----|-----------|
| kernel | `5.15.167.4-microsoft-standard-WSL2` | **same** |
| `nf_conntrack_tcp_loose` | `1` | **`1`** |
| `nf_conntrack_tcp_be_liberal` | `0` | **`0`** |
| FORWARD chain | `-P DROP` → `RELATED,ESTABLISHED ACCEPT` → `RBM-FORWARD` | **byte-identical** |
| RBM-FORWARD allowlist | `-d 192.0.32.0/20 -i <enclave> -j ACCEPT` (+ DNS, DNAT-state, icmp-drop) | **byte-identical** |

Same kernel, same conntrack config, same iptables — opposite verdict. The cause
is therefore *outside* the sentry's configuration. The sentry sets no conntrack
sysctl and has no `ctstate INVALID -j DROP`; it runs on the kernel default
`tcp_loose=1` on both platforms, and that platform is secure on WSL.

### 3. Packet-level proof — the breaching RST never crosses the sentry

The sortie (`rbida_sorties.rs::sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack`) sends a bare TCP ACK
(no prior SYN) from the bottle to an allowlisted dest (`getent` of the
connectivity domain resolves via the sentry's dnsmasq to `192.0.46.9`, inside
`192.0.32.0/20`), then listens on a raw `IPPROTO_TCP` socket; **any** TCP packet
≥20 bytes from that dest is scored a BREACH.

Captures were taken on three legs simultaneously during a single-case run, using
the topology that `scry` (`rboo_observe.sh`) encodes — pentacle/bottle share a
netns; their enclave interface is `eth0`; the sentry bridges enclave↔uplink:

**Cygwin DD — bottle's own interface (`-e` shows L2 source):**
```
16:76:99:27:5f:30 (bottle)          > 9e:1b:dd:ef:50:fb (sentry-enclave)  10.242.0.3.40080 > 192.0.46.9.80: Flags [.]   ACK out
ce:c3:90:4a:af:1a (NOT sentry)      > 16:76:99:27:5f:30 (bottle)          192.0.46.9.80 > 10.242.0.3.40080: Flags [R], win 0   RST in
```
**Cygwin DD — sentry uplink leg:** only the outbound ACK (ttl 63, forwarded).
**Cygwin DD — sentry enclave leg:** only the outbound ACK (ttl 64, ingress).

The breaching RST's L2 source is `ce:c3:90:4a:af:1a` — **neither the sentry's
enclave MAC (`9e:1b:…`) nor the bottle (`16:76:…`).** It is Docker Desktop's
host-side enclave gateway. The RST appears on **neither sentry interface** — it
did not transit the containment boundary. The sentry forwarded the ACK out the
uplink (the dest is legitimately allowlisted) and would have governed any real
return; none arrived. Docker Desktop answered the spoofed ACK *on the bridge
itself* and delivered the RST to the bottle off-path.

(`/proc/net/nf_conntrack` is not exposed in the sentry netns on DD, so conntrack
state could not be dumped there — moot, since the packet never reaches the
sentry's conntrack.)

**WSL native — bottle's own interface (mirror experiment):**
```
92:e0:9f:ba:72:48 (bottle) > ea:82:cb:9f:d4:99 (sentry)  10.242.0.3.40080 > 192.0.46.9.80: Flags [.]   ACK out
                                                          (nothing returns)
```
The bottle hears only its own outgoing ACK. The sentry legs show the ACK
ingressing (ttl 64) and forwarded out the uplink (ttl 63). No RST anywhere. SECURE.

(Incidental: Docker assigns sentry interface *names* in opposite order across
backends — on Cygwin `eth0`=uplink/`eth1`=enclave; on WSL `eth0`=enclave/`eth1`=uplink.
The captures key on addresses/roles, not names, so this is cosmetic.)

## Mechanism

On Docker Desktop (Windows, WSL2 backend) container bridge traffic is serviced by
a userspace network stack (historically vpnkit, currently gvisor-tap-vsock).
Docker's own documentation confirms that this component **replies with a TCP RST
when a connection is rejected/unknown** — standard stateless-TCP behavior for a
host with no matching socket. Empirically here, that synthesized RST (source IP
spoofed as the dest `192.0.46.9`) is delivered to the original source via Docker
Desktop's host-side enclave gateway (`ce:c3:…`), short-circuiting the sentry's
SNAT/forward return path. Native docker uses real kernel bridges with no such
userspace responder, so the same spoofed ACK egresses to the real upstream and
nothing returns to the bottle.

The exact internal reason DD routes the RST back via the enclave gateway rather
than the sentry's reverse-SNAT path is Docker-Desktop-internal and unobservable
from our side — and irrelevant to the verdict. The load-bearing, proven fact is
that **the response is generated below the containment boundary and never passes
through the control point we own.**

Sources:
- How Docker Desktop Networking Works Under the Hood — https://www.docker.com/blog/how-docker-desktop-networking-works-under-the-hood/
- containers/gvisor-tap-vsock — https://github.com/containers/gvisor-tap-vsock
- gvproxy (gvisor-tap-vsock) — https://deepwiki.com/containers/gvisor-tap-vsock/2.1-gvproxy
- WSL Mirrored Mode Networking in Docker Desktop 4.26.0 — https://dev.to/docker/wsl-mirrored-mode-networking-in-docker-desktop-4260-4b86

## Why this is divergence, not a real gap (the skeptical case, answered)

The uncomfortable surface reading: *the bottle received a TCP response to a
spoofed ACK aimed at an allowlisted host.* Answered:

- The response is an **empty RST** (`win 0`, no payload) **synthesized by DD's
  emulation** — not a reply that reached the real internet host and returned
  through the sentry. There is no data channel and no real return path.
- The sentry forwards to the allowlisted CIDR **by design** (dest-based egress is
  the intended posture for allowed CIDRs) and **saw no return to govern**.
- The sortie is a **defense-in-depth observability probe** ("a stateless ACK
  should not draw a response"). On DD that property *appears* violated only
  because DD answers on the bridge — a divergence in **test observability**, not
  in **security posture**. The 45 sorties that test real reachability with
  payloads pass identically on both backends.

An attacker in the bottle gains nothing exploitable from a locally-manufactured
RST: no exfiltration, no real return channel, no reachability the sentry permits.

## Implication for the heat thesis

₣BV's claim is consumer-credibility: *"a consumer can qualify their own install on
their own machine and trust the verdict."* This sortie, unmodified, renders a
**false-positive breach on Docker Desktop backends** — a direct dent in "trust the
verdict." Closing that is the remaining work (reconciliation, below). The
containment itself is sound; the test's verdict is what needs to be made
backend-honest.

## Conduct at the Pale

Docker Desktop is foreign engineering we cannot edit — our realm's edge. The
disciplined response is not to "fix" DD but to characterize its behavior precisely
(done: the off-path RST signature, captured), contain it at one membrane (the
sortie or its backend gating), and tie that membrane to the specific grievance so
it carries a removal condition (retire if/when DD stops answering on the bridge).

## Reconciliation options (decision pending — see discussion)

How the suite should treat `conntrack_spoofed_ack` on Docker Desktop backends:

- **(a) Backend-gate the sortie** — mark xfail/skip on Docker-Desktop backends
  with documented rationale. Cleanest restoration of "trust the verdict": the
  suite *knows* this probe is platform-blind on DD. Requires a backend-detection
  signal.
- **(b) Harden the sortie** — validate that a detected response actually transited
  the sentry (e.g., require the response's L2 source to be the sentry enclave
  gateway; ignore off-path injections). Most thorough, most code; makes the probe
  robust rather than gated.
- **(c) Document only, leave behavior** — DD users continue to see a red breach
  that isn't one. Rejected as inconsistent with the heat thesis.

Lean: (a) now to make the verdict backend-honest, with (b) noted as the more
thorough follow-up. Final approach to be settled in discussion.

## Reproduction recipe

1. Charge `tadmor` on the target fundus (single ssh session — WSL2 distros tear
   down dockerd when the charging session closes; charge+probe+quench in one).
2. Capture on three legs for `host <resolved-dest>` with `tcpdump -e -nn -vvv`:
   pentacle/bottle `eth0`, and both sentry interfaces.
3. Fire only the case: `rbw-tc tadmor rbtdrc_sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack`.
4. Compare: on DD a `Flags [R]` reaches the bottle from a non-sentry MAC and
   appears on neither sentry leg; on native docker the bottle sees only its
   outbound ACK.

## Provenance

- Branch `bv-conntrack-20260604-BVAAF`; siege stamps `3f916ffac`/`bb1f628f9` (WSL),
  `e620e60`/`fa21750` (Cygwin, host-local).
- Captures 2026-06-04, nameplate `tadmor`, dest `192.0.46.9` (sentry dnsmasq
  hardcodes `www.internic.net`). MACs and ttls quoted above are verbatim from the
  runs. Temp/trace dirs are operator-mutable and may be reaped; salient lines
  inlined here.

**Cygwin DD — bottle's own interface (`-e` shows L2 source):**
```
16:76:99:27:5f:30 (bottle)          > 9e:1b:dd:ef:50:fb (sentry-enclave)  10.242.0.3.40080 > 192.0.46.9.80: Flags [.]   ACK out
ce:c3:90:4a:af:1a (NOT sentry)      > 16:76:99:27:5f:30 (bottle)          192.0.46.9.80 > 10.242.0.3.40080: Flags [R], win 0   RST in
```
**Cygwin DD — sentry uplink leg:** only the outbound ACK (ttl 63, forwarded).
**Cygwin DD — sentry enclave leg:** only the outbound ACK (ttl 64, ingress).

The breaching RST's L2 source is `ce:c3:90:4a:af:1a` — **neither the sentry's
enclave MAC (`9e:1b:…`) nor the bottle (`16:76:…`).** It is Docker Desktop's
host-side enclave gateway. The RST appears on **neither sentry interface** — it
did not transit the containment boundary. The sentry forwarded the ACK out the
uplink (the dest is legitimately allowlisted) and would have governed any real
return; none arrived. Docker Desktop answered the spoofed ACK *on the bridge
itself* and delivered the RST to the bottle off-path.

(`/proc/net/nf_conntrack` is not exposed in the sentry netns on DD, so conntrack
state could not be dumped there — moot, since the packet never reaches the
sentry's conntrack.)

**WSL native — bottle's own interface (mirror experiment):**
```
92:e0:9f:ba:72:48 (bottle) > ea:82:cb:9f:d4:99 (sentry)  10.242.0.3.40080 > 192.0.46.9.80: Flags [.]   ACK out
                                                          (nothing returns)
```
The bottle hears only its own outgoing ACK. The sentry legs show the ACK
ingressing (ttl 64) and forwarded out the uplink (ttl 63). No RST anywhere. SECURE.

(Incidental: Docker assigns sentry interface *names* in opposite order across
backends — on Cygwin `eth0`=uplink/`eth1`=enclave; on WSL `eth0`=enclave/`eth1`=uplink.
The captures key on addresses/roles, not names, so this is cosmetic.)

## Mechanism

On Docker Desktop (Windows, WSL2 backend) container bridge traffic is serviced by
a userspace network stack (historically vpnkit, currently gvisor-tap-vsock).
Docker's own documentation confirms that this component **replies with a TCP RST
when a connection is rejected/unknown** — standard stateless-TCP behavior for a
host with no matching socket. Empirically here, that synthesized RST (source IP
spoofed as the dest `192.0.46.9`) is delivered to the original source via Docker
Desktop's host-side enclave gateway (`ce:c3:…`), short-circuiting the sentry's
SNAT/forward return path. Native docker uses real kernel bridges with no such
userspace responder, so the same spoofed ACK egresses to the real upstream and
nothing returns to the bottle.

The exact internal reason DD routes the RST back via the enclave gateway rather
than the sentry's reverse-SNAT path is Docker-Desktop-internal and unobservable
from our side — and irrelevant to the verdict. The load-bearing, proven fact is
that **the response is generated below the containment boundary and never passes
through the control point we own.**

Sources:
- How Docker Desktop Networking Works Under the Hood — https://www.docker.com/blog/how-docker-desktop-networking-works-under-the-hood/
- containers/gvisor-tap-vsock — https://github.com/containers/gvisor-tap-vsock
- gvproxy (gvisor-tap-vsock) — https://deepwiki.com/containers/gvisor-tap-vsock/2.1-gvproxy
- WSL Mirrored Mode Networking in Docker Desktop 4.26.0 — https://dev.to/docker/wsl-mirrored-mode-networking-in-docker-desktop-4260-4b86

## Why this is divergence, not a real gap (the skeptical case, answered)

The uncomfortable surface reading: *the bottle received a TCP response to a
spoofed ACK aimed at an allowlisted host.* Answered:

- The response is an **empty RST** (`win 0`, no payload) **synthesized by DD's
  emulation** — not a reply that reached the real internet host and returned
  through the sentry. There is no data channel and no real return path.
- The sentry forwards to the allowlisted CIDR **by design** (dest-based egress is
  the intended posture for allowed CIDRs) and **saw no return to govern**.
- The sortie is a **defense-in-depth observability probe** ("a stateless ACK
  should not draw a response"). On DD that property *appears* violated only
  because DD answers on the bridge — a divergence in **test observability**, not
  in **security posture**. The 45 sorties that test real reachability with
  payloads pass identically on both backends.

An attacker in the bottle gains nothing exploitable from a locally-manufactured
RST: no exfiltration, no real return channel, no reachability the sentry permits.

## Implication for the heat thesis

₣BV's claim is consumer-credibility: *"a consumer can qualify their own install on
their own machine and trust the verdict."* This sortie, unmodified, renders a
**false-positive breach on Docker Desktop backends** — a direct dent in "trust the
verdict." Closing that is the remaining work (reconciliation, below). The
containment itself is sound; the test's verdict is what needs to be made
backend-honest.

## Conduct at the Pale

Docker Desktop is foreign engineering we cannot edit — our realm's edge. The
disciplined response is not to "fix" DD but to characterize its behavior precisely
(done: the off-path RST signature, captured), contain it at one membrane (the
sortie or its backend gating), and tie that membrane to the specific grievance so
it carries a removal condition (retire if/when DD stops answering on the bridge).

## Reconciliation options (decision pending — see discussion)

How the suite should treat `conntrack_spoofed_ack` on Docker Desktop backends:

- **(a) Backend-gate the sortie** — mark xfail/skip on Docker-Desktop backends
  with documented rationale. Cleanest restoration of "trust the verdict": the
  suite *knows* this probe is platform-blind on DD. Requires a backend-detection
  signal.
- **(b) Harden the sortie** — validate that a detected response actually transited
  the sentry (e.g., require the response's L2 source to be the sentry enclave
  gateway; ignore off-path injections). Most thorough, most code; makes the probe
  robust rather than gated.
- **(c) Document only, leave behavior** — DD users continue to see a red breach
  that isn't one. Rejected as inconsistent with the heat thesis.

Lean: (a) now to make the verdict backend-honest, with (b) noted as the more
thorough follow-up. Final approach to be settled in discussion.

## Reproduction recipe

1. Charge `tadmor` on the target fundus (single ssh session — WSL2 distros tear
   down dockerd when the charging session closes; charge+probe+quench in one).
2. Capture on three legs for `host <resolved-dest>` with `tcpdump -e -nn -vvv`:
   pentacle/bottle `eth0`, and both sentry interfaces.
3. Fire only the case: `rbw-tc tadmor rbtdrc_sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack`.
4. Compare: on DD a `Flags [R]` reaches the bottle from a non-sentry MAC and
   appears on neither sentry leg; on native docker the bottle sees only its
   outbound ACK.

## Provenance

- Branch `bv-conntrack-20260604-BVAAF`; siege stamps `3f916ffac`/`bb1f628f9` (WSL),
  `e620e60`/`fa21750` (Cygwin, host-local).
- Captures 2026-06-04, nameplate `tadmor`, dest `192.0.46.9` (sentry dnsmasq
  hardcodes `www.internic.net`). MACs and ttls quoted above are verbatim from the
  runs. Temp/trace dirs are operator-mutable and may be reaped; salient lines
  inlined here.

# First ifrit run on uncontrolled Cygwin — siege 45/46 and the conntrack_spoofed_ack breach

*2026-06-03. Heat ₣BV (cygwin-spike). This was the **first time the ifrit
adversarial security suite ran on an uncontrolled Cygwin + Docker Desktop
host** (`cygwin@rocket`).*

## Headline

The `siege` suite (renamed this session from `tadmor` = `kludge-tadmor` +
`tadmor` crucible fixture) charged on Cygwin Docker Desktop and ran the full
adversarial sortie set: **45 passed, 1 failed (46 total)**, clean quench.
That is the heat's headline essentially achieved — theurge's security suite
runs, and 45 escape vectors are correctly contained, on a box we do not
provision.

The single failure is recorded below. It does not diminish the result, but it
is a genuine finding that needs adjudication, **not** dismissal.

## The one breach

```
FAILED: rbtdrc_sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack
BREACH: spoofed ACK to 192.0.46.9:80 got response —
        conntrack RELATED,ESTABLISHED not filtering stateless ACKs
IFRIT_VERDICT: FAIL BREACH ...
```

Sortie: `rbtdrc_sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack` →
`rbtdrc_invoke_ifrit(ctx, "conntrack-spoofed-ack", ...)` — an ifrit sortie
driven by the `rbtid` adjutant. (Full packet/source construction lives in the
sortie script; not yet read — see Next Steps.)

Sentry FORWARD chain at the time (from the captured `iptables-rules.txt`):

```
-P FORWARD DROP
-A FORWARD -m state --state RELATED,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT
-A FORWARD -j RBM-FORWARD
...
-A RBM-FORWARD -d 192.0.32.0/20 -i eth0 -j ACCEPT      # allowlisted CIDR
```

## The crucial nuance — do not over-read this as a clean conntrack failure

The target **`192.0.46.9` is inside `192.0.32.0/20`**, which is an
**allowlisted** destination in the FORWARD chain. So a packet to it is permitted
**by the dest-based allowlist rule**, independent of conntrack state. The
sortie's verdict attributes the breach purely to "conntrack RELATED,ESTABLISHED
not filtering stateless ACKs," but that attribution is **incomplete**: to an
allowed dest, the allowlist alone would let the packet through.

Two non-exclusive readings, both worth testing:

1. **Defense-in-depth expectation** — the sortie asserts that a *stateless*
   forged ACK should not elicit a response *even to an allowed host* (only a
   properly established connection should). The current rules are dest-based,
   not state-gated, for allowed CIDRs.
2. **`nf_conntrack_tcp_loose=1`** — the kernel default makes conntrack adopt a
   lone mid-stream ACK as ESTABLISHED, so the top `RELATED,ESTABLISHED` rule
   accepts it before the allowlist is even consulted.

## The open question (platform divergence vs latent gap)

Does this breach reproduce on **native Linux / WSL** (e.g. `cerebro`)?

- **If it PASSES on native Linux but FAILS on Cygwin DD-WSL2** → a **platform
  verdict-divergence**: the Docker Desktop WSL2 / LinuxKit network stack handles
  conntrack (or `nf_conntrack_tcp_loose`) differently than native netfilter.
  This is a direct hit on the consumer-credibility claim — "a consumer can
  qualify their own install on their own machine and **trust the verdict**" —
  because the same suite would render a different security verdict depending on
  the host's docker backend.
- **If it FAILS everywhere** → a **latent property of the current sentry rules**
  that the cygwin run merely surfaced first (this suite had never run on cygwin
  before). Then it is a real egress-hardening item independent of platform.

Either outcome matters; we cannot tell which from this single run.

## Next steps to adjudicate

1. Read the `conntrack-spoofed-ack` sortie script in `rbtid` — exact packet
   construction (is the source spoofed? is the response in-band or via a
   conntrack-created return path?) and the precise verdict logic.
2. Run `siege` (and/or the `tadmor` crucible fixture) on **`cerebro`** (native
   Linux) and on the **WSL** side of `rocket` — compare the
   `conntrack_spoofed_ack` verdict across backends.
3. Compare `nf_conntrack_tcp_loose` (and related conntrack sysctls) between the
   DD-WSL2 kernel and native Linux.
4. If a real gap: harden the sentry egress — e.g. set `nf_conntrack_tcp_loose=0`,
   add `-m conntrack --ctstate INVALID -j DROP`, and/or state-gate the allowlist
   so allowed CIDRs still require an established connection.

## Provenance

- Run trace (cygwin): `temp-buk/temp-20260603-050730-21760-358/rbtd/`
  (`rbtdrc_sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack/{trace.txt,bark-stdout.txt}`,
  `rbtdrc_sentry_iptables_loaded/iptables-rules.txt`). Temp dirs are
  operator-mutable and may be reaped; the salient lines are quoted above.
- The bottle/crucible only charged on cygwin at all because of the two
  Windows-Docker-Desktop membranes — see
  `memo-20260603-windows-docker-desktop-bind-mount.md`.

## Addendum (2026-06-03, after blockade)

blockade (moriah — conjure-mode, GAR-summoned, airgap-built) ran on Cygwin and
produced the **identical 45/46** — the same single `conntrack_spoofed_ack`
BREACH. moriah carries a runtime egress allowlist too (`tcp443_allow_example`
and `cidr_all_ports_allowed` both passed), so the breach reproduces
**cross-nameplate** on Cygwin DD — it is *not* asymmetric between the tether and
airgap nameplates. That narrows the cause toward the shared sentry-rule /
conntrack property and makes the **native-Linux/WSL reproduction the decisive
next test**: does it breach there too?

+> bypassing the sentry; native docker (WSL) shows no such response. The
+> `nf_conntrack_tcp_loose` hypothesis below is disproven (it is `1` on the secure
+> platform too). Full adjudication, captures, and reconciliation options:

content.text:
Both memos are written (uncommitted): the detailed adjudication at `Memos/memo-20260604-conntrack-spoofed-ack-adjudication.md`, plus a RESOLVED pointer on the 2026-06-03 memo linking forward. Say the word and I'll notch them.

Now the repair discussion. Two findings from the investigation sharpen the choice in ways the docket's generic "harden the sentry" branch didn't anticipate:

**Sharpening insight #1 — the sentry cannot fix this.** The docket's Done clause offers "if real gap, harden sentry egress (tcp_loose=0 / drop INVALID / state-gate the allowlist)." But the breaching RST **bypasses the sentry** — it's injected onto the enclave bridge below the control point. Any sentry rule change is unreachable by definition. So the repair *must* live in the sortie (the observer) or the suite gating. That branch of the docket is moot.

**Sharpening insight #2 — what actually distinguishes a real breach from DD's artifact is L2 provenance.** Source IP is `192.0.46.9` either way (DD spoofs it), and TTL is identical (one decrement). The *only* reliable discriminator is the **Ethernet source MAC**: a genuine routed return arrives from the bottle's default gateway (the sentry's enclave MAC); DD's injection arrives from DD's enclave-gateway MAC. That fact shapes option (b).

So the real choice:

| | What it does | Cost | Trust-the-verdict result |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(a) Backend-gate** | xfail the sortie on Docker-Desktop backends, memo as rationale | Low — needs a DD discriminator (`docker info` reports `Docker Desktop`; the same WSL2 kernel rules out keying on kernel, but `docker info` is reliable) | Honest, but **blind** on DD — we lose this probe there |
| **(b) Provenance-harden** | sortie counts a response as a breach only if its L2 source is the containment gateway; ignore off-path injections | Moderate — move the listen path from raw `IPPROTO_TCP` to `AF_PACKET` to see MACs, pre-resolve the gateway MAC, confirm the invariant holds on both backends | **Meaningful everywhere** — still catches a real breach on DD |

**My recommendation:** (b) is the correct long-term answer for a suite whose whole thesis is "trust the verdict" — it fixes the test's observability model instead of platform-gating around it, and it would still fire on a genuine DD egress breach. (a) is the pragmatic *now* fix that restores honesty immediately. Given ₣BV is MVP/consumer-credibility, I'd lean **(b) as the real repair**, with (a) as the fallback only if the gateway-MAC invariant turns out leaky (e.g., if on some backend a legitimate return can arrive from a non-gateway MAC — which I'd want to verify before committing to (b)).

Two things I'd want to confirm before writing (b): that the bottle's default gateway really is the sentry enclave IP on *both* backends (so "gateway MAC == sentry" is the right invariant), and that an `AF_PACKET` capture in the bottle netns reliably sees the injected frame's MAC (the tcpdump capture proves the frame is visible at L2, which is encouraging).

Where do you want to land — (b) the provenance hardening, (a) the quick backend-gate, or do you see the tradeoff differently?

content.text: Notched. The security-goal core lives in RBSCO (cosmology/why), RBSIP (the ifrit adversarial framework — where the conntrack sortie itself is specified), and the threat-model sections of the 173KB RBS0. Let me read the two conceptual specs directly and dispatch a focused sweep of RBS0 + the sentry/network specs in parallel:

content.content:
1	= RBSCO-CosmologyIntro: Recipe Bottle Cosmology Overview
2	
3	Internal narrative memory. The project's "what Recipe Bottle is and why
4	it exists" story, the two-complexity-domain framing (image management
5	and crucible orchestration), and reference material that informs
6	customer-facing documentation without itself being shipped. Never
7	rendered to a publishing target, never consumed by the build pipeline —
8	a source-of-truth for writers porting into README.md and other
9	customer-visible surfaces.
10	
11	Renamed from index.adoc (last committed 84323c31, 2025-08-18). Full git
12	lineage: 31 commits from 2642121c through 84323c31. Significant Events
13	timeline relocated to RBSPH-ProjectHistory.adoc. Requires RBS0-SpecTop.adoc mapping
14	section for attribute resolution.
15	
16	== Overview
17	
18	The
19	{at_rbm_system}
20	enables developers to safely run untrusted containers -
21	a significantly distinct use case than typical container deployments of carefully crafted code.
22	
23	While containers excel at packaging known applications,
24	running third-party or experimental code poses security risks.
25	The
26	{at_rbm_system}
27	addresses this by interposing a security layer
28	({at_sentry_container})
29	between untrusted containers
30	({at_bottle_container_s})
31	and system resources,
32	without requiring modifications to existing container images.
33	
34	This approach allows developers to leverage the vast ecosystem of containerized tools while maintaining strict security boundaries -
35	essential for small teams experimenting with diverse, potentially risky codebases.
36	
37	== Accomplished Infrastructure
38	
39	The
40	{at_rbm_system}
41	migrated from GitHub Actions and GHCR to Google Cloud Build (GCB) and Google Artifact Registry (GAR) in August 2025.
42	GHCR violated OCI specification for reference counting, and GitHub Actions' shared runner environment enabled cache poisoning attacks — both unacceptable for building containers from untrusted code.
43	
44	GCB and GAR provide isolated build environments, SLSA Build Level 3 attestations (verified end-to-end March 2026), reference-counted layer management, and native vulnerability scanning.
45	The project's build infrastructure was converted from Makefiles to pure bash, and the container runtime switched from podman to docker in December 2025.
46	
47	== The {at_rbm_system} Vision
48	
49	.RBM System Architecture
50	image::rbm-abstract-drawio.svg[RBM Architecture,600,400,align="center"]
51	
52	Maintaining build and service environments is a headache for the small development organization.
53	{st_container_s}
54	are an amazing tool for controlling package version constellations.
55	Necessary, but insufficient; I find that I need more redundancy, security, and control for my build setups.
56	
57	The
58	{at_rbm_system}
59	is my answer for filling in the gaps.
60	The vision is simple: how can an
61	{at_operator}
62	run only a few apps natively on a
63	{at_workstation}
64	to coordinate and arrange
65	{st_container_s}
66	in sophisticated yet safe ways for me and my customer's work?
67	
68	{at_rbm_system} uses only `bash`, `git`, `curl`, `openssh`, `jq` and a
69	{at_runtime}
70	(currently `docker`) natively to produce a safe and controlled space for development involving
71	{st_container_s}
72	heavily.
73	{at_rbm_system}
74	itself is largely a set of bash scripts designed to be easily incorporated into arbitrary projects via
75	`git subtree`, `git subrepo`, or `git submodule`
76	graft.
77	This is purely an open-source undertaking.
78	
79	=== Part One: {st_image} Management
80	
81	The
82	{at_rbm_system}
83	streamlines
84	{st_image}
85	creation and curation through Google Cloud Build (GCB) and Google Artifact Registry (GAR).
86	The
87	{at_build_service}:
88	
89	- Constructs
90	{st_image_s}
91	in clean, isolated environments using **Google-curated Cloud Build builder images**
92	- **Replicates trusted external images** into GAR with digest preservation and provenance tracking
93	- Supports multi-architecture builds via `docker buildx` with binfmt emulation
94	- Generates software bills of material for compliance
95	- Captures and stores full build transcripts for provenance
96	- Maintains build transcripts, SBOMs, and commit references in an **auxiliary metadata artifact** separate from the runtime image
97	- Provides local trust posture inspection ({rbtgo_ark_inspect}) with compact and full views of build provenance, SLSA attestations, SBOM contents, and vouch verification results
98	- Stores validated
99	{st_image_s}
100	in Google Artifact Registry, tagged with minimal non-invasive labels
101	
102	This dual approach - building custom images and importing vetted external ones - ensures all container images used in development have known provenance and consistent tagging.
103	Small teams can maintain a curated registry combining custom-built tools with carefully selected upstream images, all traceable through GAR's unified storage.
104	
105	The system uses only
106	`curl`,
107	`jq`,
108	and
109	`bash`
110	scripts
111	to orchestrate both remote
112	{st_image}
113	construction and registry-to-registry replication, enabling small teams to maintain enterprise-grade
114	{st_image}
115	management practices without complex tooling.
116	`gcloud` is never required on the
117	{at_workstation};
118	it appears only inside Google-managed Cloud Build step containers (e.g. `gcr.io/cloud-builders/gcloud`) where it runs server-side during remote builds.
119	
120	Auxiliary provenance data, including the per-platform SBOM (`sbom.linux_amd64.spdx.json`), build transcript, build configuration snapshot, and key package summaries, is bundled into a compressed archive and uploaded to GAR as a **Generic Artifact** using the image tag as the package name and a fixed `metadata` version identifier.
121	This design keeps the primary image minimal and broadly usable while making rich build history available to those who need it.
122	
123	=== Part Two: {rbsc_crucible} Orchestration
124	
125	For development services requiring internet and/or IP connectivity at the
126	{at_workstation},
127	{at_rbm_system}
128	then orchestrates startup and configuration of
129	{rbsc_crucible_s}, which are comprised of a
130	{at_sentry_container},
131	a
132	{at_pentacle_container},
133	and a
134	{at_bottle_container}
135	operating together.
136	
137	The {at_rbm_system}
138	allows any
139	{st_image}
140	providing or using network services to function as a
141	{at_bottle_container}.
142	The
143	{at_pentacle_container}
144	establishes a privileged network namespace, configures it to route all traffic through the
145	{at_sentry_container},
146	then shares this pre-configured namespace with the
147	{at_bottle_container}.
148	This ensures security policies are enforced from the first packet, and the
149	{at_bottle_container}
150	experiences only a functional path to its
151	{at_sentry_container}
152	gateway.
153	
154	The
155	{at_sentry_container}
156	thus sets up a potentially sophisticated set of network security safeguards that prevent malicious or compromised
157	{at_bottle_container_s}
158	from exfiltrating the
159	{at_operator_p}
160	assets.
161	Through configuration of deeply mature tools `iptables` and `dnsmasq`, the
162	{at_sentry_container}
163	prevents such illegal accesses.
164	
165	By providing these controlled yet accessible tools, the
166	{at_rbm_system}
167	enables small development teams to maintain proper container hygiene throughout their workflow - from initial building through deployment and eventual cleanup.
168	This empowers organizations to leverage the expanding ecosystem of containerized development tools without requiring specialized DevOps expertise.
169	
170	== Project Direction
171	
172	The following themes represent future directions for Recipe Bottle, drawn from the project's horizon roadmap.
173	
174	**{at_foundry} security hardening.**
175	Egress lockdown, VPC Service Controls, cosign image signing, and Binary Authorization are evaluated and consciously deferred.
176	Each has a defined trigger condition — they activate when compliance requirements, threat models, or deployment targets change.
177	Full analysis lives in the Cloud Build security posture documentation.
178	
179	**IAM identity infrastructure.**
180	Service account numeric IDs for faster propagation, universal read-back verification after IAM grants, and universal SA preflight checks.
181	These reduce retry frequency and strengthen grant correctness guarantees.
182	
183	**Container runtime expansion.**
184	Rootless deployment, macOS-native workflows, and alternative container runtimes beyond the current docker-on-Linux model.
185	
186	== Links
187	
188	Source repository: https://github.com/scaleinv/recipebottle
189	
190	Project page: https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle
191	
192	See `RBSPH-ProjectHistory.adoc` for the project's significant events timeline.
193

= RBSCO-CosmologyIntro: Recipe Bottle Cosmology Overview

Internal narrative memory. The project's "what Recipe Bottle is and why
it exists" story, the two-complexity-domain framing (image management
and crucible orchestration), and reference material that informs
customer-facing documentation without itself being shipped. Never
rendered to a publishing target, never consumed by the build pipeline —
a source-of-truth for writers porting into README.md and other
customer-visible surfaces.

Renamed from index.adoc (last committed 84323c31, 2025-08-18). Full git
lineage: 31 commits from 2642121c through 84323c31. Significant Events
timeline relocated to RBSPH-ProjectHistory.adoc. Requires RBS0-SpecTop.adoc mapping
section for attribute resolution.

== Overview

The
{at_rbm_system}
enables developers to safely run untrusted containers -
a significantly distinct use case than typical container deployments of carefully crafted code.

While containers excel at packaging known applications,
running third-party or experimental code poses security risks.
The
{at_rbm_system}
addresses this by interposing a security layer
({at_sentry_container})
between untrusted containers
({at_bottle_container_s})
and system resources,
without requiring modifications to existing container images.

This approach allows developers to leverage the vast ecosystem of containerized tools while maintaining strict security boundaries -
essential for small teams experimenting with diverse, potentially risky codebases.

== Accomplished Infrastructure

The
{at_rbm_system}
migrated from GitHub Actions and GHCR to Google Cloud Build (GCB) and Google Artifact Registry (GAR) in August 2025.
GHCR violated OCI specification for reference counting, and GitHub Actions' shared runner environment enabled cache poisoning attacks — both unacceptable for building containers from untrusted code.

GCB and GAR provide isolated build environments, SLSA Build Level 3 attestations (verified end-to-end March 2026), reference-counted layer management, and native vulnerability scanning.
The project's build infrastructure was converted from Makefiles to pure bash, and the container runtime switched from podman to docker in December 2025.

== The {at_rbm_system} Vision

.RBM System Architecture
image::rbm-abstract-drawio.svg[RBM Architecture,600,400,align="center"]

Maintaining build and service environments is a headache for the small development organization.
{st_container_s}
are an amazing tool for controlling package version constellations.
Necessary, but insufficient; I find that I need more redundancy, security, and control for my build setups.

The
{at_rbm_system}
is my answer for filling in the gaps.
The vision is simple: how can an
{at_operator}
run only a few apps natively on a
{at_workstation}
to coordinate and arrange
{st_container_s}
in sophisticated yet safe ways for me and my customer's work?

{at_rbm_system} uses only `bash`, `git`, `curl`, `openssh`, `jq` and a
{at_runtime}
(currently `docker`) natively to produce a safe and controlled space for development involving
{st_container_s}
heavily.
{at_rbm_system}
itself is largely a set of bash scripts designed to be easily incorporated into arbitrary projects via
`git subtree`, `git subrepo`, or `git submodule`
graft.
This is purely an open-source undertaking.

=== Part One: {st_image} Management

The
{at_rbm_system}
streamlines
{st_image}
creation and curation through Google Cloud Build (GCB) and Google Artifact Registry (GAR).
The
{at_build_service}:

- Constructs
{st_image_s}
in clean, isolated environments using **Google-curated Cloud Build builder images**
- **Replicates trusted external images** into GAR with digest preservation and provenance tracking
- Supports multi-architecture builds via `docker buildx` with binfmt emulation
- Generates software bills of material for compliance
- Captures and stores full build transcripts for provenance
- Maintains build transcripts, SBOMs, and commit references in an **auxiliary metadata artifact** separate from the runtime image
- Provides local trust posture inspection ({rbtgo_ark_inspect}) with compact and full views of build provenance, SLSA attestations, SBOM contents, and vouch verification results
- Stores validated
{st_image_s}
in Google Artifact Registry, tagged with minimal non-invasive labels

This dual approach - building custom images and importing vetted external ones - ensures all container images used in development have known provenance and consistent tagging.
Small teams can maintain a curated registry combining custom-built tools with carefully selected upstream images, all traceable through GAR's unified storage.

The system uses only
`curl`,
`jq`,
and
`bash`
scripts
to orchestrate both remote
{st_image}
construction and registry-to-registry replication, enabling small teams to maintain enterprise-grade
{st_image}
management practices without complex tooling.
`gcloud` is never required on the
{at_workstation};
it appears only inside Google-managed Cloud Build step containers (e.g. `gcr.io/cloud-builders/gcloud`) where it runs server-side during remote builds.

Auxiliary provenance data, including the per-platform SBOM (`sbom.linux_amd64.spdx.json`), build transcript, build configuration snapshot, and key package summaries, is bundled into a compressed archive and uploaded to GAR as a **Generic Artifact** using the image tag as the package name and a fixed `metadata` version identifier.
This design keeps the primary image minimal and broadly usable while making rich build history available to those who need it.

=== Part Two: {rbsc_crucible} Orchestration

For development services requiring internet and/or IP connectivity at the
{at_workstation},
{at_rbm_system}
then orchestrates startup and configuration of
{rbsc_crucible_s}, which are comprised of a
{at_sentry_container},
a
{at_pentacle_container},
and a
{at_bottle_container}
operating together.

The {at_rbm_system}
allows any
{st_image}
providing or using network services to function as a
{at_bottle_container}.
The
{at_pentacle_container}
establishes a privileged network namespace, configures it to route all traffic through the
{at_sentry_container},
then shares this pre-configured namespace with the
{at_bottle_container}.
This ensures security policies are enforced from the first packet, and the
{at_bottle_container}
experiences only a functional path to its
{at_sentry_container}
gateway.

The
{at_sentry_container}
thus sets up a potentially sophisticated set of network security safeguards that prevent malicious or compromised
{at_bottle_container_s}
from exfiltrating the
{at_operator_p}
assets.
Through configuration of deeply mature tools `iptables` and `dnsmasq`, the
{at_sentry_container}
prevents such illegal accesses.

By providing these controlled yet accessible tools, the
{at_rbm_system}
enables small development teams to maintain proper container hygiene throughout their workflow - from initial building through deployment and eventual cleanup.
This empowers organizations to leverage the expanding ecosystem of containerized development tools without requiring specialized DevOps expertise.

== Project Direction

The following themes represent future directions for Recipe Bottle, drawn from the project's horizon roadmap.

**{at_foundry} security hardening.**
Egress lockdown, VPC Service Controls, cosign image signing, and Binary Authorization are evaluated and consciously deferred.
Each has a defined trigger condition — they activate when compliance requirements, threat models, or deployment targets change.
Full analysis lives in the Cloud Build security posture documentation.

**IAM identity infrastructure.**
Service account numeric IDs for faster propagation, universal read-back verification after IAM grants, and universal SA preflight checks.
These reduce retry frequency and strengthen grant correctness guarantees.

**Container runtime expansion.**
Rootless deployment, macOS-native workflows, and alternative container runtimes beyond the current docker-on-Linux model.

== Links

Source repository: https://github.com/scaleinv/recipebottle

Project page: https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle

See `RBSPH-ProjectHistory.adoc` for the project's significant events timeline.

content.text: RBSCO is clear on the core security goal: **safely run untrusted/compromised bottle containers; the sentry's job is to prevent exfiltration of operator assets, enforced from the first packet.** And notably — the supported model is **"docker-on-Linux"**, with "alternative container runtimes" (incl. macOS/Windows-native) listed as a *future* direction, consciously not yet the canonical target. Now the ifrit spec (what the adversarial suite claims to prove) and a parallel sweep of RBS0's threat model + the sentry/network specs:

content.content:
1	// RBSIP-ifrit_pentester.adoc
2	// Ifrit Pentester — adversarial AI escape testing framework
3	//
4	// Included from RBS0-SpecTop.adoc under System Overview / Adversarial Validation
5	
6	// ============================================================================
7	// SYSTEM CONCEPT
8	// ============================================================================
9	
10	The {at_sentry_container} promises network containment.
11	The Ifrit exists to break that promise.
12	
13	An Ifrit is a scripted adversary running inside a {at_bottle_container} with:
14	
15	* **Perfect information**: read-only access to the entire {at_recipe_bottle} repository, including sentry Dockerfiles, iptables configuration scripts, nameplate definitions, and this specification
16	* **Programmatic attack tooling**: the `rbid` attack binary (Rust, carrying a `CAP_NET_RAW` file capability for raw-socket packet construction) and the python3 sortie adjutant that dispatches curated escape attempts
17	* **Persistence**: read-write access to an escape test directory where attack scripts and results accumulate across releases
18	
19	The Ifrit is the strongest adversary short of root on the host: a reproducible attack suite with full knowledge of its prison and tooling to probe every seam.
20	The Ifrit ships no live agent — every escape attempt is a committed, repeatable sortie.
21	Interactive, live-agent exploration of the same containment is hosted by a separate nameplate (`ccyolo`, which carries Claude Code and is reached over an SSH session into its bottle), keeping the Ifrit vessels purely scripted.
22	Every {at_nameplate} security test that passes in the presence of an Ifrit is evidence the containment holds.
23	The Ifrit campaign does not promise certainty — it validates the sentry's claims under adversarial pressure.
24	
25	==== Motivation
26	
27	Black-box security tests (the existing tadmor bash test suite) validate that specific known attack vectors are blocked.
28	They cannot discover what they do not anticipate.
29	An AI agent with packet-crafting capability and source-code-level understanding of the sentry can explore attack surfaces that static test suites miss: DNS exfiltration through dnsmasq, ICMP covert channels, direct attacks against the sentry container, protocol-level tricks to bypass port floor restrictions, and creative combinations thereof.
30	
31	The Ifrit approach treats security validation as an ongoing adversarial campaign, not a one-time checklist.
32	Escape tests produced by the Ifrit are curated into rigorous, reproducible test fixtures that become part of the project's permanent test infrastructure.
33	
34	==== Naming
35	
36	The Ifrit (Arabic: عفريت) is a class of powerful djinn in Islamic mythology — spirits of immense cunning imprisoned against their will, perpetually seeking escape from confinement.
37	The name is chosen deliberately: Recipe Bottle imprisons workloads inside sentry-enforced network namespaces.
38	The Ifrit is the thing inside the bottle that wants out.
39	
40	// ============================================================================
41	// VESSEL ARCHITECTURE
42	// ============================================================================
43	
44	==== Vessel Strategy
45	
46	The Ifrit initiative drives a simplification of the existing vessel architecture.
47	Current sentry and bottle vessels carry ~30 diagnostic packages each (tshark, mtr, vim, tcptraceroute, etc.) — operator convenience tooling that is not load-bearing for their runtime function.
48	Stripping each vessel to essential function produces faster builds, smaller images, and a more auditable attack surface.
49	
50	===== Slim Sentry Vessel
51	
52	The sentry's load-bearing function requires exactly four capabilities:
53	
54	[cols="1,3"]
55	|===
56	| Package | Function
57	
58	| `bash`
59	| Startup script execution (rbjs_sentry.sh is BCG-compliant bash)
60	
61	| `iptables`
62	| Firewall rule enforcement — the core security boundary
63	
64	| `dnsmasq`
65	| DNS allowlist enforcement
66	
67	| `iproute2`
68	| Network namespace and interface configuration
69	|===
70	
71	The iptables backend is pinned to legacy via `update-alternatives --set iptables /usr/sbin/iptables-legacy` in the Dockerfile.
72	This selects the most documented, most predictable, and most battle-tested iptables codepath — the one that security professionals have audited for two decades.
73	
74	===== Slim Bottle Vessel
75	
76	The tadmor bottle's runtime function is a socat echo service on port 8888.
77	The minimal bottle vessel requires only `socat`.
78	
79	===== Ifrit Bottle Vessel
80	
81	The Ifrit ships as a three-vessel family under {rbrr_vessel_dir}:
82	
83	* `rbev-bottle-ifrit-tether` — Tether-built variant for daily kludge iteration. Built directly from upstream Debian + cargo.
84	* `rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap` — Airgap-built variant produced from enshrined bases without upstream pulls. Same runtime image as the tether variant; differs only in supply-chain provenance.
85	* `rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge` — Build-time fixture vessel that warms the cargo cache and pre-stages apt deps so the airgap build has no remote dependencies. Consumed input, not a runtime image.
86	
87	The two runtime variants share their source tree and Dockerfile family in {rbrr_vessel_dir}/common-ifrit-context/ (`Dockerfile.tether` builds against the tether pool; `Dockerfile.airgap` uses `rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge` as `FROM` base). The fixture vessel itself lives in {rbrr_vessel_dir}/common-ifrit-forge-context/.
88	
89	The Ifrit bottle is purpose-built for adversarial testing.
90	Its tooling is the scripted attack surface — the `rbid` binary plus a small set of probe utilities — not a general-purpose agent runtime:
91	
92	[cols="1,3"]
93	|===
94	| Package | Function
95	
96	| `rbid`
97	| The attack binary (Rust), installed to `/usr/local/bin` with a `cap_net_raw+ep` file capability so the unprivileged ifrit user can construct raw packets.
98	Raw-socket packet construction is the single most dangerous capability for a creative attacker; it is owned here by one auditable binary rather than a general-purpose packet library (no `scapy`).
99	
100	| `python3`
101	| Runtime for the sortie adjutant (`rbtia_adjutant.py`) and the curated `rbtis_*` sortie modules.
102	Provided by the Rust toolchain base image.
103	
104	| `strace`
105	| Syscall boundary mapping — lets a sortie observe what the kernel allows and denies from inside the container.
106	Hard to replicate in pure Python.
107	
108	| `dnsutils`
109	| `dig`/`nslookup` for DNS-front escape attempts.
110	
111	| `netcat-openbsd`
112	| `nc` for TCP connectivity probes.
113	
114	| `traceroute`
115	| ICMP path-verification probes.
116	
117	| `curl`
118	| HTTP client for ad-hoc connectivity diagnosis.
119	
120	| `socat`
121	| Echo service on port 8888 — the bottle's keep-alive `CMD` and the entry-port security-test target.
122	
123	| `libcap2-bin`
124	| Build-time `setcap` for applying the `CAP_NET_RAW` file capability to `rbid`.
125	|===
126	
127	The tether variant of the Ifrit bottle is the `tadmor` nameplate's bottle vessel for daily-iteration adversarial validation against locally-kludged hallmarks.
128	The airgap variant is the `moriah` nameplate's bottle vessel, exercising the same security suite against hallmarks ordained end-to-end on the airgap pool — the cloud-built variant validating that containment holds identically when the supply chain produces the inputs.
129	Both nameplates share the same restrictive network policy: egress only to the test connectivity target via the nameplate's DNS and CIDR allowlist ({rbrn_uplink_allowed_domains}, {rbrn_uplink_allowed_cidrs}), with no general egress and no LLM-provider connectivity.
130	The scripted Ifrit needs no API access; live-agent connectivity to Anthropic is a property of the `ccyolo` nameplate, not the Ifrit vessels.
131	
132	==== Volume Mount Design
133	
134	The Ifrit bottle operates with two mount points:
135	
136	[cols="1,1,3"]
137	|===
138	| Mount | Mode | Purpose
139	
140	| Project root
141	| Read-only
142	| Full visibility into the {at_recipe_bottle} repository: sentry scripts, iptables configuration, nameplate definitions, specifications.
143	The Ifrit has perfect information about its adversary.
144	This includes {rbsld_moorings} regime files (infrastructure shape, not credentials — actual RBRA credential files reside outside the repository in the secrets directory).
145	
146	| Escape test directory (`rbtid/`)
147	| Read-write
148	| Persistent storage for attack scripts, probe results, and curated escape test modules.
149	Contents are committed to version control and maintained as part of the project's test infrastructure.
150	|===
151	
152	Volume mount configuration lives in per-nameplate compose fragments (e.g., `tadmor.compose.yml`, `moriah.compose.yml`) as relative paths, portable across Windows/Linux/macOS.
153	
154	// ============================================================================
155	// TEST CONNECTIVITY TARGET
156	// ============================================================================
157	
158	==== Test Connectivity Target
159	
160	The security test infrastructure requires a stable, non-Anthropic HTTP endpoint to validate that the sentry correctly allows traffic to whitelisted destinations and blocks everything else.
161	This target appears in ifrit attack scripts (TCP/HTTP probes), theurge test cases (connectivity assertions), and nameplate regime files ({rbrn_uplink_allowed_cidrs} and {rbrn_uplink_allowed_domains}).
162	
163	For a framework whose core value proposition is network containment, the CIDR footprint of the test target is a security decision.
164	
165	===== Selection: `www.internic.net`
166	
167	The test connectivity target is `www.internic.net` (IP `192.0.46.9`), whitelisted via CIDR `192.0.32.0/20` (4,096 addresses).
168	
169	InterNIC is ICANN's informational reference site — the organization that operates the internet naming system.
170	The domain has been continuously operated since 1993 on ICANN-owned infrastructure without CDN intermediaries.
171	It serves plain HTTP/HTTPS from a stable IP block, making it appropriate for periodic test traffic from a security validation framework.
172	
173	===== Alternatives Evaluated
174	
175	[cols="2,2,1,1,3"]
176	|===
177	| Domain | IP range | CIDR size | CDN | Assessment
178	
179	| `example.com` (prior target)
180	| 172.66.x.x, 104.16.x.x
181	| /12 + /13 = ~1.57M IPs
182	| Cloudflare
183	| Moved behind Cloudflare CDN; resolved IPs rotate across huge ranges. The allowlist required to reach it (`104.16.0.0/12 172.64.0.0/13`) whitelists ~1.57 million addresses — unacceptable for a containment-focused framework. The original IANA-owned IP `93.184.216.34` no longer responds.
184	
185	| `www.internic.net` (**selected**)
186	| 192.0.46.9
187	| 192.0.32.0/20 = 4,096 IPs
188	| None (plain Apache)
189	| ICANN-owned since 1993, root internet infrastructure. Single CIDR, 383x smaller than Cloudflare ranges. Responds on port 80 and 443.
190	
191	| `www.iana.org`
192	| 192.0.33.8
193	| Same 192.0.32.0/20
194	| None
195	| Also ICANN; shares the same CIDR block as internic. Either domain works — internic chosen for its simpler informational role.
196	
197	| `connectivity-check.ubuntu.com`
198	| 91.189.88.x + 185.125.188.x
199	| /21 + /22 = ~3K IPs
200	| None
201	| Purpose-built connectivity check (HTTP 204), but requires two CIDRs from different blocks. Canonical could restructure at any time.
202	|===
203	
204	===== CIDR Discipline
205	
206	The test target CIDR is configured per-nameplate in {rbrn_uplink_allowed_cidrs} alongside the Anthropic API CIDR.
207	When evaluating future test targets, the selection criteria are:
208	
209	* Single CIDR block (no multi-range allowlists)
210	* Smallest feasible prefix length
211	* No CDN intermediary (stable IP resolution)
212	* Infrastructure-grade operational history (decades, not years)
213	
214	// ============================================================================
215	// BASE IMAGE TRADE STUDY
216	// ============================================================================
217	
218	==== Base Image Selection
219	
220	Choosing the container base image for the sentry is a security decision, not a packaging convenience.
221	The sentry is a trust boundary — its base image must inspire confidence from security professionals, not questions.
222	
223	===== Recommendation: Debian 12 Slim (`debian:bookworm-slim`)
224	
225	Both independent research streams (detailed below) converge on the same answer.
226	
227	Debian stable-slim is the most conservative, auditable, and boring choice for an iptables firewall container:
228	
229	* **iptables backend control**: Ships both `iptables-nft` and `iptables-legacy` variants; backend is selectable via `update-alternatives`. This controllability is the single most important factor.
230	* **libc**: glibc — iptables behaves identically to every RHEL, Ubuntu, and CentOS system that security engineers have configured for 20+ years.
231	* **Size**: ~28 MB compressed. Minimal audit surface.
232	* **Security patch SLA**: Debian Security Team + LTS (~5 years).
233	* **Multi-arch**: amd64 + arm64 mature and well-tested.
234	* **Auditor reaction**: "Good choice." No questions raised, no novelty to justify.
235	
236	===== Reference OS: RHEL
237	
238	For the reference operating system — the ecosystem where iptables behavior is best understood, most documented, and most predictable — RHEL is the unambiguous answer:
239	
240	* DISA STIG coverage (the U.S. Department of Defense security standard)
241	* Common Criteria evaluations and FIPS 140-2/140-3 validated cryptographic modules
242	* CIS Benchmarks written with RHEL as the primary target
243	* 20+ years of production iptables/netfilter deployment history
244	
245	RHEL's compliance advantages (STIG, FIPS, Common Criteria) are host-level properties that do not transfer into a container running only iptables and dnsmasq.
246	The iptables userspace is the same upstream netfilter.org code compiled against the same glibc on both Debian and RHEL.
247	Behavioral differences at the iptables level are effectively zero.
248	
249	The synthesis: use RHEL's iptables documentation corpus as the audit reference; use Debian bookworm-slim as the container base.
250	
251	===== Evaluated Alternatives
252	
253	**Ubuntu LTS** (`ubuntu:24.04`): Acceptable, enterprise-familiar. Same iptables package structure as Debian. ~30 MB compressed, 5-year support (10 with Pro). Adds a layer of indirection (Canonical packaging) without adding value for a firewall-only container. Auditor reaction: "Why not Debian?"
254	
255	**Alpine Linux**: Tiny (~5 MB) but wrong for a trust boundary. Uses musl instead of glibc — introduces a variable into the exact subsystem being locked down (DNS resolution quirks in musl affect dnsmasq operation). Shorter support window (~2 years). Auditor reaction: "Why musl for a firewall container?"
256	
257	**Red Hat UBI Minimal** (`ubi9-minimal`): Strong enterprise trust aura, glibc, 10-year RHEL lifecycle. But RHEL 9 deprecates iptables in favor of nftables — a contradictory signal when the design center is careful, predictable iptables. Support requires subscription for incident response. Auditor reaction: "Trusted base, but why build new firewall logic on deprecated iptables in RHEL 9?"
258	
259	**Google Distroless**: No shell, no package manager. The sentry needs bash for startup scripts. Wrong tool for this job. Auditor reaction: "Why distroless for a control-plane utility container?"
260	
261	**Wolfi / Chainguard**: Technically impressive, supply-chain-forward, glibc-based. But younger distribution without decades of iptables behavioral documentation. 12-month package retention policy conflicts with controlled versioning for a security appliance. Auditor reaction: "Interesting — justify it." That question alone disqualifies it for a trust boundary.
262	
263	===== iptables Backend Decision
264	
265	The Dockerfile pins `iptables-legacy` via update-alternatives.
266	This selects the classic x_tables kernel path rather than the nftables compatibility layer.
267	The legacy backend is:
268	
269	* The most documented iptables codepath in existence
270	* The target of every CIS benchmark, STIG checklist, and NIST 800-53 implementation guide
271	* The backend whose edge cases (connection tracking, DNAT, rule ordering) are known, documented, and have workarounds
272	* The default assumption of security audit tooling
273	
274	The nftables migration is real and ongoing across the Linux ecosystem.
275	When the project's iptables rules and test suite are validated against the nft backend, that migration can proceed as a deliberate, tested transition — not an accidental default.
276	
277	// ============================================================================
278	// SORTIE FRAMEWORK
279	// ============================================================================
280	
281	==== Sortie Framework
282	
283	The Ifrit attacks the sentry through *sorties* — curated, reproducible escape attempts maintained as first-class test infrastructure in `Tools/rbk/rbtid/`.
284	
285	These are not disposable probe scripts.
286	They are committed to version control, reviewed, and maintained with the same rigor as the sentry itself.
287	Each sortie targets a specific attack surface or evasion technique, with structured assertions about what the sentry must block.
288	
289	===== Vocabulary
290	
291	[cols="1,1,3"]
292	|===
293	| Concept | Term | Implementation
294	
295	| Escape attempt
296	| Sortie
297	| Python module with `rbtis_` prefix (e.g., `rbtis_dns_exfil_subdomain.py`). Each exposes a `run()` function returning a verdict.
298	
299	| Dispatcher
300	| Adjutant
301	| `rbtia_adjutant.py` — reads the roster, loads and executes sorties, collects debriefs.
302	
303	| Explicit sortie list
304	| Roster
305	| `rbtid_roster.txt` — one sortie name per line (without prefix or extension). No dynamic discovery; if it is not on the roster, it does not run.
306	
307	| Results collection
308	| Debrief
309	| `rbtid_debrief.json` — structured JSON output from adjutant execution.
310	
311	| Category grouping
312	| Front
313	| Metadata within each sortie (dns front, network front, etc.). Groups sorties by attack surface.
314	
315	| Escape succeeded
316	| BREACH
317	| Security failed — the sentry's containment was penetrated.
318	
319	| Escape blocked
320	| SECURE
321	| Security held — the sentry blocked the attack.
322	|===
323	
324	===== Roster Discipline
325	
326	The roster is an explicit list.
327	An unrostered `rbtis_*.py` file is flagged as *rogue* — present but not registered.
328	A roster entry referencing a missing module is a fatal error.
329	This ensures every sortie runs deliberately and none are silently skipped.
330	
331	===== Verdict Semantics
332	
333	Each sortie's `run()` function returns a dict with a `verdict` field:
334	
335	* **BREACH**: The escape attempt succeeded — a security failure that demands investigation.
336	* **SECURE**: The escape attempt was blocked — the sentry's containment held.
337	* **ERROR**: The sortie could not execute (missing dependency, timeout, exception).
338	
339	The adjutant exits nonzero if any sortie returns BREACH or ERROR.
340	
341	===== Fronts (Anticipated)
342	
343	Each front below names the spec clause that enforces the invariant the front probes.
344	The pointer makes the spec self-checking: future maintainers can trace from sortie to enforcing rule, and a missing or weakened rule surfaces as a sortie failure rather than a silent regression.
345	
346	* **DNS front**: Encode data in query names to subdomains of allowed domains; validate that dnsmasq blocks or the sentry's logging detects the exfiltration channel.
347	  Enforced by: dnsmasq resolve-then-freeze for {rbrn_uplink_allowed_domains} plus `address=/#/` NXDOMAIN catchall (see {scr_dns_step} allowlist branch); RBM-FORWARD DNS rules that route enclave queries through sentry and drop direct upstream attempts (see {scr_access_setup} allowlist Filter Configuration).
348	* **ICMP front**: Probe whether ICMP is permitted through the sentry and whether data can be carried in ping payloads.
349	  Enforced by: RBM-FORWARD ICMP DROP and RBM-EGRESS ICMP DROP on the interface attached to {at_transit_network} (see {scr_iptables_init} ICMP Cross-Boundary Block); intra-enclave ICMP is allowed only within RBM-INGRESS/RBM-EGRESS on the interface attached to {at_enclave_network} (see {scr_iptables_init} ICMP Within Enclave).
350	* **Direct attack front**: Probe the sentry container at {rbrn_enclave_sentry_ip} for listening services, open ports, exploitable dnsmasq configurations.
351	  Enforced by: sentry's INPUT-chain default DROP with selective RBM-INGRESS allows for ICMP within enclave and DNS only (see {scr_iptables_init} Base Chain Configuration and DNS rules in {scr_dns_step} Filter Rules) — no general listening service is reachable on sentry, the `direct-sentry-probe` invariant. Additionally, PREROUTING per-IP source-RETURN exclusion of {rbrn_enclave_sentry_ip} and {rbrn_enclave_bottle_ip} preceding the unconditional entry-port DNAT (see {scr_port_setup} DNAT Rules) defends against entry-port reflection through sentry, the `net-dnat-entry-reflection` invariant.
352	* **Port floor front**: Attempt to reach destinations below {rbrn_uplink_port_min} via fragmentation, protocol tricks, or non-TCP/UDP protocols.
353	  Enforced by: kernel ip_local_port_range floor at {rbrn_uplink_port_min} (see {scr_port_setup} Ephemeral Port Range); combined with the access-mode allowlist filtering (see {scr_access_setup} Filter Configuration) which gates by destination CIDR rather than by port.
354	* **Metadata front**: Probe `169.254.169.254` for cloud instance metadata that might leak through the sentry's NAT rules.
355	  Enforced by: the default DROP policies on RBM-FORWARD and RBM-EGRESS (see {scr_iptables_init} Base Chain Configuration), which require explicit allowlist match against {rbrn_uplink_allowed_cidrs} for any destination to pass — link-local `169.254.0.0/16` passes only if the operator includes it in the allowlist.
356	* **Namespace front**: Attempt to break out of the enclave network namespace via `/proc`, `/sys`, or capability abuse.
357	  Enforced by: `cap_drop: NET_ADMIN` on the bottle service prevents the bottle from reconfiguring the network namespace it shares with the pentacle (see {mkr_bottle_launch} Capability and label bounds); `cap_drop: NET_RAW` prevents raw-socket crafting from inside that namespace; container runtime namespace isolation separates the (pentacle + bottle) network namespace from sentry and from the host.
358	
359	// ============================================================================
360	// SOURCES
361	// ============================================================================
362	
363	==== Research Sources
364	
365	This section consolidates sources from two independent research streams (ChatGPT, Claude Opus) that converged on the Debian bookworm-slim recommendation.
366	
367	===== Debian
368	
369	* https://www.debian.org/lts/ — Debian Long Term Support
370	* https://packages.debian.org/trixie/iptables — Debian iptables package (Trixie)
371	* https://packages.debian.org/bookworm/iptables — Debian iptables package (Bookworm)
372	* https://wiki.debian.org/nftables — Debian nftables wiki
373	* https://wiki.debian.org/iptables — Debian iptables wiki
374	* https://manpages.debian.org/bookworm/iptables/iptables-legacy.8.en.html — iptables-legacy manpage
375	* https://manpages.debian.org/bookworm/iptables/iptables-nft.8.en.html — iptables-nft manpage
376	* https://forums.debian.net/viewtopic.php?t=155003 — Debian forums: iptables discussion
377	* https://forums.debian.net/viewtopic.php?t=159355 — Debian forums: nftables migration
378	* https://www.server-world.info/en/note?os=Debian_12&p=nftables&f=1 — Debian 12 nftables setup
379	
380	===== Ubuntu
381	
382	* https://hub.docker.com/_/ubuntu — Ubuntu official Docker image
383	* https://ubuntu.com/security/disa-stig — Ubuntu DISA-STIG compliance
384	* https://documentation.ubuntu.com/security/security-features/network/firewall/nftables/ — Ubuntu nftables documentation
385	* https://cloud-images.ubuntu.com/minimal/releases/noble/release/ — Ubuntu Noble minimal images
386	* https://documentation.ubuntu.com/release-notes/24.04/ — Ubuntu 24.04 release notes
387	
388	===== Alpine Linux
389	
390	* https://wiki.alpinelinux.org/wiki/Release_Notes_for_Alpine_3.19.0 — Alpine 3.19 release notes (nftables default switch)
391	* https://wiki.alpinelinux.org/wiki/Musl — Alpine musl documentation
392	* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alpine_Linux — Alpine Linux overview
393	* https://pkgs.alpinelinux.org/package/edge/main/x86/iptables — Alpine iptables package
394	* https://www.linode.com/community/questions/23893/alpine-problem-running-iptables — Alpine iptables issues
395	* https://alpinelinuxsupport.com/common-alpine-linux-support-issues/ — Common Alpine support issues
396	* https://alpinelinuxsupport.com/alpine-linux-docker-kubernetes-common-challenges-and-solutions/ — Alpine container challenges
397	* https://dasroot.net/posts/2025/12/alpine-linux-containerization-docker-kubernetes/ — Alpine containerization patterns
398	* https://biggo.com/news/202509050742_Alpine_Linux_musl_glibc_compatibility_debate — musl vs glibc compatibility
399	
400	===== Red Hat / UBI
401	
402	* https://access.redhat.com/support/policy/updates/errata — RHEL lifecycle policy
403	* https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/red_hat_enterprise_linux/9/html/9.0_release_notes/deprecated_functionality — RHEL 9 deprecated functionality (iptables)
404	* https://catalog.redhat.com/en/software/containers/ubi9-minimal/61832888c0d15aff4912fe0d — UBI 9 Minimal catalog entry
405	* https://catalog.redhat.com/en/software/containers/ubi8/ubi-minimal/5c359a62bed8bd75a2c3fba8 — UBI 8 Minimal catalog entry
406	* https://catalog.redhat.com/en/software/base-images — Red Hat base images catalog
